name: मोक्षसंन्यासयोग
teaching: The whole Gita returns in a final spiral: act, but release the fruit; honour your own dharma, even done imperfectly, over another's done well; let devotion carry what effort cannot. Then the teaching ends as all true teaching must — 'thus I have declared it; reflect fully, and do as you choose.' And when every support fails, the last word: abandon all dharmas, take refuge in Me alone; do not grieve. Everything converges: renunciation redefined as release of fruit, work itself made worship, every secret crowned by the final one — abandon all supports and come; I will carry it all. The student stands, the teaching ends in freedom, and where God and human effort ride together, victory is certain.
questions: What is your svadharma — the work that is yours even when done imperfectly?
Krishna ends with 'do as you choose.' What does it mean that the Gita's last move is to return your freedom?
Having walked all eighteen chapters: what, now, will you actually do — like Arjuna, whose final words are 'my doubt is gone; I will act'?

verses.speaker: अर्जुन उवाच
verses.devanagari:
संन्यासस्य महाबाहो तत्त्वमिच्छामि वेदितुम् ।
त्यागस्य च हृषीकेश पृथक्केशिनिषूदन॥
verses.transliteration:
saṃnyāsasya mahābāho tattvamicchāmi veditum
tyāgasya ca hṛṣīkeśa pṛthakkeśiniṣūdana
verses.essence: Arjuna asks: I wish to know severally, Mahabaho, the truth of renunciation, Hrishikesha — and of relinquishment, Keshinishudana.
verses.translation: I desire to know severally, O mighty-armed, the essence or truth of renunciation, O Hrishikesa, as also of abandonment, O slayer of Kesi.

anvaya.meaning: the truth I wish to know

verses.explanation: The final chapter opens with the question beneath all the others: saṁnyāsa and tyāga — renouncing acts versus renouncing within acts — pṛthak, told apart. It is chapter 1's dilemma matured: Arjuna once wanted to walk away from the war; now he asks, with a scholar's precision, what walking away even means. The Gita's whole summation will unfold from this distinction.
verses.connection: The last question — refining ch5's sannyasa-vs-karma-yoga debate into its final form.
verses.reflection: Bring your oldest question to its mature form today: not 'should I quit?' but 'what is quitting?'
verses.background: The final chapter opens with Arjuna's last question, the one the whole dialogue began with in another form: I wish to know the truth of sannyasa and of tyaga, O mighty-armed — renunciation and relinquishment, severally.

verses.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
काम्यानां कर्मणां न्यासं संन्यासं कवयो विदुः ।
सर्वकर्मफलत्यागं प्राहुस्त्यागं विचक्षणाः॥
verses.transliteration:
kāmyānāṃ karmaṇāṃ nyāsaṃ saṃnyāsaṃ kavayo viduḥ
sarvakarmaphalatyāgaṃ prāhustyāgaṃ vicakṣaṇāḥ
verses.essence: The Blessed Lord says: the sages know renunciation as the giving up of desire-driven works; the relinquishment of the fruit of all action, the discerning call relinquishment.
verses.translation: The sages understand Sannyasa to be the renunciation of action with desire; the wise declare the abandonment of the fruits of all actions as Tyaga.

anvaya.meaning: the relinquishing of all action's fruit

verses.explanation: Two clean definitions: saṁnyāsa drops the kāmya — the optional, appetite-driven projects; tyāga keeps acting but drops the fruit of *everything*. The first prunes the tree of deeds; the second changes the sap in all of it. The chapter will side decisively with the second — the renunciation you can carry into a battle, a kitchen, an office.
verses.connection: Answers 18.1's distinction — and quietly crowns tyaga, the Gita's signature move since 2.47.
verses.schools_note: The opening definitions — sannyasa as dropping desire-born acts, tyaga as dropping all fruits — get school-specific weights: Shankara holds full sannyasa (renunciation of action itself, in knowledge) as the final stage the chapter still honors; Ramanuja reads the Gita as ranking inner tyaga above outer abandonment for nearly all; Jnaneshwar speaks for the karma-yoga line: the robe is optional, the release of fruit is not.
verses.reflection: Practice both definitions once today: drop one optional appetite-project, and drop the fruit of one duty.
verses.background: Krishna settles the old dispute by definition and verdict: sages call sannyasa the giving up of desire-prompted acts, tyaga the giving up of the fruit of all acts. Some say all action should be abandoned as evil; others exempt sacrifice, gift, and austerity. Hear My settled judgment: acts of sacrifice, gift, and austerity must not be abandoned — they purify the wise; but even these are to be done releasing attachment and fruit. The threefold tyaga is graded: from delusion, tamasic; from fear of bodily trouble, rajasic; duty done with attachment and fruit renounced, sattvic.

verses.devanagari:
त्याज्यं दोषवदित्येके कर्म प्राहुर्मनीषिणः ।
यज्ञदानतपःकर्म न त्याज्यमिति चापरे॥
verses.transliteration:
tyājyaṃ doṣavadityeke karma prāhurmanīṣiṇaḥ
yajñadānatapaḥkarma na tyājyamiti cāpare
verses.essence: Some thinkers declare that action should be abandoned as a fault; others say that acts of sacrifice, charity and austerity are never to be abandoned.
verses.translation: Some philosophers declare that actions should be abandoned as an evil; while others (declare) that acts of sacrifice, gift and austerity should not be relinished.

anvaya.meaning: 'to be abandoned, as faulty' — so some
the acts of sacrifice, charity, austerity

verses.explanation: Before ruling, Krishna stages the debate honestly: one school holds all action defective — every deed casts a shadow — therefore quit; another holds the three great purifiers exempt. The Gita's method on display: opposing positions stated fairly, then transcended rather than merely refereed.
verses.connection: The ancient dispute laid out, so 18.4–6 can deliver the verdict.
verses.reflection: Before your next verdict, state the opposing case as fairly as Krishna states this one.
verses.background: Krishna settles the old dispute by definition and verdict: sages call sannyasa the giving up of desire-prompted acts, tyaga the giving up of the fruit of all acts. Some say all action should be abandoned as evil; others exempt sacrifice, gift, and austerity. Hear My settled judgment: acts of sacrifice, gift, and austerity must not be abandoned — they purify the wise; but even these are to be done releasing attachment and fruit. verses.devanagari:
निश्चयं शृणु मे तत्र त्यागे भरतसत्तम ।
त्यागो हि पुरुषव्याघ्र त्रिविधः सम्प्रकीर्तितः॥
verses.transliteration:
niścayaṃ śṛṇu me tatra tyāge bharatasattama
tyāgo hi puruṣavyāghra trividhaḥ samprakīrtitaḥ
verses.essence: Hear my conclusion in this matter of relinquishment, best of Bharatas; for relinquishment, tiger among men, has been declared to be of three kinds.
verses.translation: Hear from Me the conclusion or the final truth about this abandonment, O best of the Bharatas; abandonment, verily, O best of men, has been declared to be of three kinds.

anvaya.meaning: regarding tyaga, O best of Bharatas
for tyaga, O tiger among men

verses.explanation: Niścayam — *my settled conclusion*: the teacher steps forward from the survey to rule. And the ruling instrument is the chapter's master-tool: the threefold analysis of chapters 14 and 17, now turned on renunciation itself. Even quitting, it turns out, comes in sattvic, rajasic and tamasic grades.
verses.connection: Announces the method: tyaga itself submitted to the guna-lens (18.7–9).
verses.reflection: Remember that even your quitting has a quality — grade today's renunciations honestly.

verses.devanagari:
यज्ञदानतपःकर्म न त्याज्यं कार्यमेव तत् ।
यज्ञो दानं तपश्चैव पावनानि मनीषिणाम्॥
verses.transliteration:
yajñadānatapaḥkarma na tyājyaṃ kāryameva tat
yajño dānaṃ tapaścaiva pāvanāni manīṣiṇām
verses.essence: Acts of sacrifice, charity and austerity are not to be relinquished; they must indeed be performed — for sacrifice, charity and austerity are purifiers of the wise.
verses.translation: Acts of sacrifice, gift and austerity should not be abandoned, but should be performed; sacrifice, gift and also austerity are the purifiers of the wise.

anvaya.meaning: the work of sacrifice, charity, austerity
is not to be abandoned; it is surely to be done

verses.explanation: The first half of the verdict: the three great acts stay — kāryam eva, emphatically to be done. Their defense is functional: pāvanāni, they *purify*; giving loosens the fist, worship loosens the ego, discipline loosens the appetites. Even manīṣiṇām, the wise, still need the scrubbing. No stage of the path outgrows generosity.
verses.connection: Settles 18.3's dispute for the keep-acting school — with 18.6 adding the decisive condition.
verses.reflection: Keep the three purifiers on your calendar — give, discipline, worship — no stage of life outgrows them.
verses.background: Krishna settles the old dispute by definition and verdict: sages call sannyasa the giving up of desire-prompted acts, tyaga the giving up of the fruit of all acts. Some say all action should be abandoned as evil; others exempt sacrifice, gift, and austerity. Hear My settled judgment: acts of sacrifice, gift, and austerity must not be abandoned — they purify the wise; but even these are to be done releasing attachment and fruit. verses.devanagari:
एतान्यपि तु कर्माणि सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा फलानि च ।
कर्तव्यानीति मे पार्थ निश्चितं मतमुत्तमम्॥
verses.transliteration:
etānyapi tu karmāṇi saṅgaṃ tyaktvā phalāni ca
kartavyānīti me pārtha niścitaṃ matamuttamam
verses.essence: But even these actions should be performed relinquishing attachment and fruits, Partha — this is my definite and highest judgment.
verses.translation: But even these actions should be performed leaving aside attachment and the desire for rewards, O Arjuna; this is My certain and best conviction.

anvaya.meaning: are to be done — this, O Partha, is my

verses.explanation: The complete formula, signed with full authority — niścitaṁ matam uttamam: do the works, drop two things from inside them — saṅga, the clutch of 'mine', and phala, the claim on outcome. Action's body kept, action's ego removed. Sixteen chapters of teaching distilled to one sentence the dying tradition would carve in stone.
verses.connection: The Gita's thesis in final form: 2.47 and 5.10, restated as the Lord's settled verdict.
verses.schools_note: 'Etan mama nishchitam matam uttamam' — this is My definite, highest judgment: commentators treat the phrase as the Gita formally ruling on its own oldest debate. Even Shankara, champion of renunciate knowledge, concedes the verse's force for the active life as preparation; the theistic schools read it as final doctrine: acts kept, attachment and fruit released. Few verses carry an explicit 'this is My settled view' — all schools mark it.
verses.reflection: Apply the final formula to one task today: do it fully, with attachment and fruit removed from inside it.

verses.devanagari:
नियतस्य तु संन्यासः कर्मणो नोपपद्यते ।
मोहात्तस्य परित्यागस्तामसः परिकीर्तितः॥
verses.transliteration:
niyatasya tu saṃnyāsaḥ karmaṇo nopapadyate
mohāttasya parityāgastāmasaḥ parikīrtitaḥ
verses.essence: But the renunciation of obligatory action is not proper; its abandonment through delusion is declared to be tamasic.
verses.translation: Verily the renunciation of obligatory action is not proper; the abandonment of the same from delusion is declared to be Tamasic.

anvaya.meaning: but the renunciation of the obligatory

verses.explanation: The grading of quitting begins at the bottom: dropping niyata karma — the duties woven into one's station and relationships — out of mohāt, confusion about what duty even is. The seeker who abandons dependents for 'detachment', the soldier who deserts calling it peace: renunciation as a fog mistaking itself for a summit.
verses.connection: First grade of tyaga — and a quiet verdict on the chapter-1 Arjuna who wished to flee.
verses.reflection: Check one abandoned duty for fog — was that renunciation, or confusion wearing its robe?

verses.devanagari:
दुःखमित्येव यत्कर्म कायक्लेशभयात्त्यजेत् ।
स कृत्वा राजसं त्यागं नैव त्यागफलं लभेत्॥
verses.transliteration:
duḥkhamityeva yatkarma kāyakleśabhayāttyajet
sa kṛtvā rājasaṃ tyāgaṃ naiva tyāgaphalaṃ labhet
verses.essence: One who abandons action merely because it is troublesome, from fear of bodily affliction — having made a rajasic relinquishment, one gains no fruit of relinquishment at all.
verses.translation: He who abandons action on account of the fear of bodily trouble (because it is painful), does not obtain the merit of renunciation by doing such Rajasic renunciation.

anvaya.meaning: 'it is painful' — merely thus, the action
one abandons, from fear of bodily strain
that one, having made a rajasic tyaga
gains not at all the fruit of tyaga

verses.explanation: The middle grade: quitting because the work is hard — duḥkham iti eva, 'this is unpleasant', the whole analysis. Renunciation as comfort-seeking wearing a spiritual costume. The verdict is precise: such a person performs the *act* of relinquishment and receives none of its fruit — the peace belongs to those who release the grip, not those who flee the weight.
verses.connection: Second grade: the resignation letter signed by aversion, not freedom.
verses.reflection: Catch yourself quitting something today merely because it is hard — and notice no peace arrives.

verses.devanagari:
कार्यमित्येव यत्कर्म नियतं क्रियतेऽर्जुन ।
सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा फलं चैव स त्यागः सात्त्विको मतः॥
verses.transliteration:
kāryamityeva yatkarma niyataṃ kriyate'rjuna
saṅgaṃ tyaktvā phalaṃ caiva sa tyāgaḥ sāttviko mataḥ
verses.essence: When obligatory action is performed because it ought to be done, Arjuna, relinquishing attachment and fruit — that relinquishment is held to be sattvic.
verses.translation: Whatever obligatory action is done, O Arjuna, merely because it ought to be done, abandoning attachment and also the desire for reward, that renunciation is regarded as Sattvic (pure).

anvaya.meaning: 'it ought to be done' — merely thus, the action

verses.explanation: The highest grade inverts expectation: sattvic *relinquishment* looks like showing up. Kāryam iti eva — 'it is to be done', the same clean grammar as 17.11's offering and 17.20's gift — the duty performed with both inner hooks removed. The renunciate and the worker turn out to be the same person: hands fully employed, heart fully free.
verses.connection: The chapter's keystone: true tyaga happens inside action — completing the answer to 18.1.
verses.reflection: Say the sattvic sentence over one chore: it ought to be done — then do it, hooks removed.

verses.devanagari:
न द्वेष्ट्यकुशलं कर्म कुशले नानुषज्जते ।
त्यागी सत्त्वसमाविष्टो मेधावी छिन्नसंशयः॥
verses.transliteration:
na dveṣṭyakuśalaṃ karma kuśale nānuṣajjate
tyāgī sattvasamāviṣṭo medhāvī chinnasaṃśayaḥ
verses.essence: The relinquisher pervaded by sattva, wise, whose doubts are severed, hates not disagreeable work nor clings to the agreeable.
verses.translation: The man of renunciation, pervaded by purity, intelligent, and with his doubts cut asunder, does not hate a disagreeable work nor is he attached to an agreeable one.

anvaya.meaning: to the agreeable is not attached

verses.explanation: The portrait of the sattvic relinquisher: the inner seesaw stilled — pleasant tasks no longer courted, unpleasant ones no longer dodged; the work is simply what is in front of the hands. Chinna-saṁśayaḥ, doubts severed: the long argument with one's own duty, which began on this very battlefield, finally quiet.
verses.connection: What 18.9's tyaga feels like from inside: the end of the like/dislike negotiation with one's work.
verses.reflection: Spend a day off the seesaw: neither dodging the unpleasant task nor courting the pleasant one.

Hear My settled judgment: acts of sacrifice, gift, and austerity must not be abandoned — they purify the wise; but even these are to be done releasing attachment and fruit. The threefold tyaga is graded: from delusion, tamasic; from fear of bodily trouble, rajasic; duty done with attachment and fruit renounced, sattvic.

verses.devanagari:
न हि देहभृता शक्यं त्यक्तुं कर्माण्यशेषतः ।
यस्तु कर्मफलत्यागी स त्यागीत्यभिधीयते॥
verses.transliteration:
na hi dehabhṛtā śakyaṃ tyaktuṃ karmāṇyaśeṣataḥ
yastu karmaphalatyāgī sa tyāgītyabhidhīyate
verses.essence: For an embodied being cannot relinquish actions entirely; but one who relinquishes the fruit of action — that one is called the relinquisher.
verses.translation: Verily, it is not possible for an embodied being to abandon actions entirely; but he who relinishes the rewards of actions is verily called a man of renunciation.

anvaya.meaning: for it is not possible, by a body-bearer
but who is a relinquisher of action's fruit

verses.explanation: The decisive realism: na hi dehabhṛtā śakyam — a body *is* action; breath, digestion, the beat of the heart never resign. Total quitting is not renunciation but a fiction (3.5's old point, now final doctrine). So the title 'tyāgī' is formally reassigned: not the one who does nothing, but the one who keeps nothing — karma-phala-tyāgī, the fruit-releaser.
verses.connection: Closes the definitional question of 18.1 with anatomy: only the fruit can be fully renounced.
verses.reflection: Stop attempting the impossible renunciation today — breath and heartbeat never resign. Release fruits instead.

The threefold tyaga is graded: from delusion, tamasic; from fear of bodily trouble, rajasic; duty done with attachment and fruit renounced, sattvic.

verses.devanagari:
अनिष्टमिष्टं मिश्रं च त्रिविधं कर्मणः फलम् ।
भवत्यत्यागिनां प्रेत्य न तु संन्यासिनां क्वचित्॥
verses.transliteration:
aniṣṭamiṣṭaṃ miśraṃ ca trividhaṃ karmaṇaḥ phalam
bhavatyatyāgināṃ pretya na tu saṃnyāsināṃ kvacit
verses.essence: Unwished, wished, and mixed — threefold is the fruit of action that accrues after death to the non-relinquishers; but never to the renouncers.
verses.translation: The threefold fruit of action (evil, good and mixed) accrues after death to the non-abandoners, but never to the abandoners.

anvaya.meaning: threefold is the fruit of action
accrues, to the non-relinquishers, after death
but never to the renouncers, anywhere

verses.explanation: The stakes across the death-line: for the grasper, every deed ripens — sweet, bitter, or the usual blend — and the harvest follows into the hereafter. For the fruit-releaser: na tu... kvacit, *never, anywhere*. Not because their deeds vanish, but because nothing was planted in the soil of 'mine'. An open hand, it turns out, is the only place karma cannot take root.
verses.connection: The metaphysical payoff of 18.11: the released fruit never returns to bind.
verses.reflection: Plant nothing in the soil of 'mine' today, and notice there is nothing to harvest you later.

Hear My settled judgment: acts of sacrifice, gift, and austerity must not be abandoned — they purify the wise; but even these are to be done releasing attachment and fruit. The threefold tyaga is graded: from delusion, tamasic; from fear of bodily trouble, rajasic; duty done with attachment and fruit renounced, sattvic.

verses.devanagari:
पञ्चैतानि महाबाहो कारणानि निबोध मे ।
साङ्ख्ये कृतान्ते प्रोक्तानि सिद्धये सर्वकर्मणाम्॥
verses.transliteration:
pañcaitāni mahābāho kāraṇāni nibodha me
sāṅkhye kṛtānte proktāni siddhaye sarvakarmaṇām
verses.essence: Learn from me, Mahabaho, these five causes for the accomplishment of all action, declared in the Sankhya doctrine where actions reach their end.
verses.translation: Learn from Me, O mighty-armed Arjuna, these five causes as declared in the Sankhya system for the accomplishment of all actions.

anvaya.meaning: declared in the Sankhya, where action concludes
for the accomplishment of all actions

verses.explanation: A new analysis opens: if the fruit must be released, the *authorship* must be understood. The Sankhya — the science where action's accounting is settled — is summoned to dissect a single question: when something gets done, who actually did it? Five causes are about to replace the one proud claimant.
verses.connection: Begins the doership-audit (18.13–17): the final dismantling of 'I did this'.
verses.reflection: Audit one accomplishment today for its real causes — prepare to find five names on the invoice.
verses.background: The anatomy of action is laid out from Sankhya doctrine: five factors in every deed — the body-seat, the doer, the instruments, the various functions, and providence as the fifth. Seeing this, whoever beholds the self as sole doer sees not. He whose state is free of the ego-notion, though he slay these worlds, slays not and is not bound. Knowledge, the known, and the knower impel action; instrument, act, and agent comprise it.

verses.devanagari:
अधिष्ठानं तथा कर्ता करणं च पृथग्विधम् ।
विविधाश्च पृथक्चेष्टा दैवं चैवात्र पञ्चमम्॥
verses.transliteration:
adhiṣṭhānaṃ tathā kartā karaṇaṃ ca pṛthagvidham
vividhāśca pṛthakceṣṭā daivaṃ caivātra pañcamam
verses.essence: The seat of action, the agent, the various instruments, the manifold distinct efforts, and divine providence as the fifth —
verses.translation: The seat (body), the doer, the various senses, the different functions of various sorts, and the presiding deity, also, the fifth.

anvaya.meaning: and the instruments, of various kinds
and daiva (the divine) — here the fifth

verses.explanation: The five factors of every deed: the body as platform; the apparent agent; the instruments — senses, tools, hands; the many energies actually exerting; and daivam, the fifth — the unaccountable: providence, the cooperation of circumstance, everything in the outcome that no one controls. Any honest audit of any accomplishment finds all five on the invoice.
verses.connection: The cast list behind every act — drawn up so 18.16 can expose the one false credit.
verses.schools_note: 'Daivam chaivatra panchamam' — providence as the fifth factor of every act: the Gita's formal entry of the uncontrollable into the anatomy of action. Mimamsa read daiva as the unseen ripening of past karma; the theistic schools as the Lord's superintending will; Shankara as the deities presiding over the faculties. All draw the same two morals: humility about outcomes, and the absurdity of sole credit or sole blame.
verses.reflection: Honor the fifth factor once today: daivam — the part of every outcome no one controlled.
verses.background: The anatomy of action is laid out from Sankhya doctrine: five factors in every deed — the body-seat, the doer, the instruments, the various functions, and providence as the fifth. Seeing this, whoever beholds the self as sole doer sees not. He whose state is free of the ego-notion, though he slay these worlds, slays not and is not bound. Knowledge, the known, and the knower impel action; instrument, act, and agent comprise it.

verses.devanagari:
शरीरवाङ्मनोभिर्यत्कर्म प्रारभते नरः ।
न्याय्यं वा विपरीतं वा पञ्चैते तस्य हेतवः॥
verses.transliteration:
śarīravāṅmanobhiryatkarma prārabhate naraḥ
nyāyyaṃ vā viparītaṃ vā pañcaite tasya hetavaḥ
verses.essence: Whatever action a person undertakes by body, speech and mind, whether right or contrary — these five are its causes.
verses.translation: Whatever action a man performs with his body, speech and mind whether right or the reverse these five are its causes.

anvaya.meaning: by body, speech, and mind, whatever

verses.explanation: The audit's scope declared total: every deed through any door — body, speech, mind — and of any moral color, nyāyyaṁ vā viparītaṁ vā, righteous or its opposite. No act anywhere escapes the five-fold causation; the saint's service and the thief's theft are both committee productions. Which makes the next verse's lone claimant absurd.
verses.connection: Universalizes 18.14's five causes — setting the trap for the solitary 'I'.
verses.reflection: Extend the audit everywhere: body, speech, mind; right deeds and wrong — all are committee work.
verses.background: The anatomy of action is laid out from Sankhya doctrine: five factors in every deed — the body-seat, the doer, the instruments, the various functions, and providence as the fifth. Seeing this, whoever beholds the self as sole doer sees not. He whose state is free of the ego-notion, though he slay these worlds, slays not and is not bound. Knowledge, the known, and the knower impel action; instrument, act, and agent comprise it.

verses.devanagari:
तत्रैवं सति कर्तारमात्मानं केवलं तु यः ।
पश्यत्यकृतबुद्धित्वान्न स पश्यति दुर्मतिः॥
verses.transliteration:
tatraivaṃ sati kartāramātmānaṃ kevalaṃ tu yaḥ
paśyatyakṛtabuddhitvānna sa paśyati durmatiḥ
verses.essence: That being so, one who sees the self alone as the doer — through unrefined understanding — that perverse-minded one does not truly see.
verses.translation: Now, such being the case, verily he who owing to untrained understanding looks upon his Self, which is isolated, as the agent, he of perverted intelligence, sees not.

anvaya.meaning: such being the case, as the doer
that one does not see — the wrong-minded

verses.explanation: The exposure: with five causes on record, crediting kevalam ātmānam — the bare self, alone — is not pride's harmless flourish but a perceptual error: na sa paśyati, that one *does not see*. Akṛta-buddhitvāt: the instrument of understanding never finished. Every 'I did it single-handedly' is a five-handed event wearing one glove.
verses.connection: The audit's verdict: sole-doership as the root misperception — preparing 18.17's stunning corollary.
verses.reflection: Catch one 'I did it single-handedly' today and count the other four hands.
verses.background: The anatomy of action is laid out from Sankhya doctrine: five factors in every deed — the body-seat, the doer, the instruments, the various functions, and providence as the fifth. Seeing this, whoever beholds the self as sole doer sees not. He whose state is free of the ego-notion, though he slay these worlds, slays not and is not bound. Knowledge, the known, and the knower impel action; instrument, act, and agent comprise it.

verses.devanagari:
यस्य नाहंकृतो भावो बुद्धिर्यस्य न लिप्यते ।
हत्वाऽपि स इमाँल्लोकान्न हन्ति न निबध्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
yasya nāhaṃkṛto bhāvo buddhiryasya na lipyate
hatvā'pi sa imā.Nllokānna hanti na nibadhyate
verses.essence: One who is free of the sense 'I am the doer', whose understanding is unstained — though slaying these worlds, such a one slays not, and is not bound.
verses.translation: He who is free from the egoistic notion, whose intelligence is not tainted (by good or evil), though he slays these people, he slayeth not, nor is he bound (by the action).

anvaya.meaning: whose state is without the 'I-am-doer' sense
though slaying these worlds, that one

verses.explanation: The audit's vertiginous conclusion, aimed straight at the man holding the bow: where the ahaṅkṛta bhāva — the knot of 'I am the agent' — is genuinely absent and the buddhi unstained by claim or hatred, the deed does not adhere. Hatvāpi... na hanti: even slaying, one slays not. Not a loophole but a description of the rarest inner state — and the chapter immediately fences it (18.18–25) with the full machinery of duty, lest it be mistaken for permission.
verses.connection: Echoes 2.19–21 at the chapter of decision: action without ego leaves no fingerprint.
verses.schools_note: The tradition handles this verse like a blade. Shankara: for the knower, agency itself was always unreal, so no deed binds. Ramanuja anchors it to ordained duty — only war commanded by dharma, performed without ego, is covered. All commentators agree it describes an achieved egolessness, not an attitude one may adopt to excuse a chosen violence.
verses.reflection: Aspire to the unstained state honestly — and notice the verse describes egolessness, never excuses.

verses.devanagari:
ज्ञानं ज्ञेयं परिज्ञाता त्रिविधा कर्मचोदना ।
करणं कर्म कर्तेति त्रिविधः कर्मसंग्रहः॥
verses.transliteration:
jñānaṃ jñeyaṃ parijñātā trividhā karmacodanā
karaṇaṃ karma karteti trividhaḥ karmasaṃgrahaḥ
verses.essence: Knowledge, the known and the knower form the threefold impulse to action; the instrument, the act and the agent form the threefold constitution of action.
verses.translation: Knowledge, the knowable and the knower form the threefold impulse to action; the organ, the action and the agent form the threefold basis of action.
verses.explanation: The anatomy of any deed, before and after: upstream, the triad that sparks it — something known, someone knowing, the knowing itself; downstream, the triad that constitutes it — instrument, act, agent. Six joints in every action. The chapter now runs its guna-lens over three of them — knowledge, action, agent — and then onward through the whole inner apparatus.
verses.connection: The framework for the great triads (18.19–40): everything human, sorted three ways.
verses.reflection: Watch one act assemble today: known, knower, knowing — then instrument, deed, doer. Six joints, every time.
verses.background: The anatomy of action is laid out from Sankhya doctrine: five factors in every deed — the body-seat, the doer, the instruments, the various functions, and providence as the fifth. Seeing this, whoever beholds the self as sole doer sees not. He whose state is free of the ego-notion, though he slay these worlds, slays not and is not bound. Knowledge, the known, and the knower impel action; instrument, act, and agent comprise it.

verses.devanagari:
ज्ञानं कर्म च कर्ताच त्रिधैव गुणभेदतः ।
प्रोच्यते गुणसङ्ख्याने यथावच्छृणु तान्यपि॥
verses.transliteration:
jñānaṃ karma ca kartāca tridhaiva guṇabhedataḥ
procyate guṇasaṅkhyāne yathāvacchṛṇu tānyapi
verses.essence: Knowledge, action and agent are declared in the doctrine of the gunas to be of three kinds only, according to the difference of the gunas. Hear of them also, duly.
verses.translation: Knowledge, action and actor are declared in the science of the Gunas (Sankhya philosophy) to be of three kinds only, according to the distinction of the Gunas. Of these also, hear duly.

anvaya.meaning: threefold only, by difference of guna
hear of them too, as they are

verses.explanation: The program announced: jñāna, karma, kartā — the seeing, the doing, the doer — each refracted through sattva, rajas, tamas. What follows through 18.40 is the Gita's final gift of discernment: a complete field guide to the inner life, nine portraits and more, by which a person can locate, at any hour, which strand currently holds the pen.
verses.connection: Opens the systematic survey that runs to 18.40 — the guna-science completed.
verses.reflection: Carry the field-guide consciously today: your seeing, doing, and doer each have a current grade.
verses.background: The great threefold catalogue unrolls — the chapter's longest movement: knowledge that sees the one imperishable in all beings, or only separateness, or one fragment as the whole; action obligatory and unattached, or strained by desire, or undertaken blindly; the doer free of ego, or greedy and volatile, or indolent and despairing; then intellect, firmness, and finally happiness itself — poison first and nectar after for sattva, nectar first and poison after for rajas, delusion throughout for tamas. Nothing on earth or in heaven is free of the three strands.

verses.devanagari:
सर्वभूतेषु येनैकं भावमव्ययमीक्षते ।
अविभक्तं विभक्तेषु तज्ज्ञानं विद्धि सात्त्विकम्॥
verses.transliteration:
sarvabhūteṣu yenaikaṃ bhāvamavyayamīkṣate
avibhaktaṃ vibhakteṣu tajjñānaṃ viddhi sāttvikam
verses.essence: That knowledge by which one sees the one imperishable Being in all beings — undivided in the divided — know that knowledge to be sattvic.
verses.translation: That by which one sees the one indestructible Reality in all beings, not separate in all the separate beings know thou that knowledge to be Sattvic.

anvaya.meaning: in all beings, by which the one

verses.explanation: The highest knowing in one image: avibhaktaṁ vibhakteṣu — the undivided seen *within* the divided; one ocean recognized beneath the count of waves. Sattvic knowledge does not deny the many; it sees through them without erasing them — 13.28's vision filed here as the gold standard of cognition itself.
verses.connection: First panel of the knowledge-triad: unity perceived through, not instead of, difference.
verses.reflection: Practice the gold standard of knowing once: the undivided glimpsed inside the divided crowd.
verses.background: The great threefold catalogue unrolls — the chapter's longest movement: knowledge that sees the one imperishable in all beings, or only separateness, or one fragment as the whole; action obligatory and unattached, or strained by desire, or undertaken blindly; the doer free of ego, or greedy and volatile, or indolent and despairing; then intellect, firmness, and finally happiness itself — poison first and nectar after for sattva, nectar first and poison after for rajas, delusion throughout for tamas. Nothing on earth or in heaven is free of the three strands.

verses.devanagari:
पृथक्त्वेन तु यज्ज्ञानं नानाभावान्पृथग्विधान् ।
वेत्ति सर्वेषु भूतेषु तज्ज्ञानं विद्धि राजसम्॥
verses.transliteration:
pṛthaktvena tu yajjñānaṃ nānābhāvānpṛthagvidhān
vetti sarveṣu bhūteṣu tajjñānaṃ viddhi rājasam
verses.essence: But that knowledge which sees in all beings manifold entities of distinct kinds, each separate — know that knowledge to be rajasic.
verses.translation: But that knowledge which sees in all beings various entities of distinct kinds as different from one another know thou that knowledge to be Rajasic.

anvaya.meaning: but the knowledge which, by separateness

verses.explanation: Rajasic knowing: the world as pure inventory — pṛthaktvena, by separateness first and last; each being its own sealed unit, no common thread suspected. It is competent knowledge — science, commerce and strategy run on it — but it stops at the catalogue, and a catalogued world is one to be ranked, compared, and contested.
verses.connection: Second panel: accurate about the many, blind to the one — the cognition that fuels rivalry.
verses.reflection: Notice inventory-vision running today — everything separate, rankable — competent, and blind to the thread.
verses.background: The great threefold catalogue unrolls — the chapter's longest movement: knowledge that sees the one imperishable in all beings, or only separateness, or one fragment as the whole; action obligatory and unattached, or strained by desire, or undertaken blindly; the doer free of ego, or greedy and volatile, or indolent and despairing; then intellect, firmness, and finally happiness itself — poison first and nectar after for sattva, nectar first and poison after for rajas, delusion throughout for tamas. Nothing on earth or in heaven is free of the three strands.

verses.devanagari:
यत्तु कृत्स्नवदेकस्मिन्कार्ये सक्तमहैतुकम् ।
अतत्त्वार्थवदल्पं च तत्तामसमुदाहृतम्॥
verses.transliteration:
yattu kṛtsnavadekasminkārye saktamahaitukam
atattvārthavadalpaṃ ca tattāmasamudāhṛtam
verses.essence: But that which clings to one single effect as if it were the whole — without reason, without grasp of truth, and trivial — that is declared tamasic.
verses.translation: But that which clings to one single effect as if it were the whole, without reason, without foundation in Truth, and trivial that is declared to be Tamasic.

anvaya.meaning: but that which, as if it were the whole, to one

verses.explanation: Tamasic knowing: one fragment mistaken for everything — kṛtsna-vat ekasmin, the whole world shrunk to a single obsession, grievance, or slogan; held ahaitukam, without reason, immune to evidence; alpam, small, and proud of its smallness. Fanaticism's epistemology in fourteen words.
verses.connection: Third panel: the part enthroned as the whole — knowledge collapsed to a keyhole.
verses.reflection: Find where one fragment has become your whole — an obsession, a grievance — and reopen the frame.
verses.background: The great threefold catalogue unrolls — the chapter's longest movement: knowledge that sees the one imperishable in all beings, or only separateness, or one fragment as the whole; action obligatory and unattached, or strained by desire, or undertaken blindly; the doer free of ego, or greedy and volatile, or indolent and despairing; then intellect, firmness, and finally happiness itself — poison first and nectar after for sattva, nectar first and poison after for rajas, delusion throughout for tamas. verses.devanagari:
नियतं सङ्गरहितमरागद्वेषतः कृतम् ।
अफलप्रेप्सुना कर्म यत्तत्सात्त्विकमुच्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
niyataṃ saṅgarahitamarāgadveṣataḥ kṛtam
aphalaprepsunā karma yattatsāttvikamucyate
verses.essence: Action that is ordained, free of attachment, performed without passion or hatred by one not desiring the fruit — that is called sattvic.
verses.translation: An action which is ordained, which is free from attachment, and which is done without love or hatred by one who is not desirous of any reward that action is declared to be Sattvic.

anvaya.meaning: by one not grasping for fruit — the action

verses.explanation: The action-triad opens with the deed this whole scripture commends: niyatam, the called-for thing; performed with neither the lean of craving nor the flinch of aversion; the doer aphala-prepsu, not reaching past the act toward its reward. Work as clean as weather — it happens fully, and claims nothing.
verses.connection: The sattvic deed: 18.9's tyaga seen from the action's side.
verses.reflection: Do the called-for thing today without lean or flinch — neither craving the task nor dreading it.
verses.background: The great threefold catalogue unrolls — the chapter's longest movement: knowledge that sees the one imperishable in all beings, or only separateness, or one fragment as the whole; action obligatory and unattached, or strained by desire, or undertaken blindly; the doer free of ego, or greedy and volatile, or indolent and despairing; then intellect, firmness, and finally happiness itself — poison first and nectar after for sattva, nectar first and poison after for rajas, delusion throughout for tamas. verses.devanagari:
यत्तु कामेप्सुना कर्म साहंकारेण वा पुनः ।
क्रियते बहुलायासं तद्राजसमुदाहृतम्॥
verses.transliteration:
yattu kāmepsunā karma sāhaṃkāreṇa vā punaḥ
kriyate bahulāyāsaṃ tadrājasamudāhṛtam
verses.essence: But action performed with great strain by one seeking desires, or again with the sense of 'I' — that is declared rajasic.
verses.translation: But that action which is done by one longing for the fulfilment of desires or gain with egoism or with much effort that is declared to be Rajasic (passionate).

anvaya.meaning: but the action by one craving desires

verses.explanation: Rajasic action's tell is in the texture: bahulāyāsam, *enormously effortful* — not because the task is large but because the doer is carrying two loads, the work and the self doing the work. Wanting and self-display are heavy. Notice the same deed done sattvic feels lighter; the strain was never in the labor but in the grip.
verses.connection: The straining middle: action freighted with appetite and authorship.
verses.reflection: Feel the double load once today — the work plus the self doing the work — and set down the second.
verses.background: The great threefold catalogue unrolls — the chapter's longest movement: knowledge that sees the one imperishable in all beings, or only separateness, or one fragment as the whole; action obligatory and unattached, or strained by desire, or undertaken blindly; the doer free of ego, or greedy and volatile, or indolent and despairing; then intellect, firmness, and finally happiness itself — poison first and nectar after for sattva, nectar first and poison after for rajas, delusion throughout for tamas. Nothing on earth or in heaven is free of the three strands.

verses.devanagari:
अनुबन्धं क्षयं हिंसामनपेक्ष्य च पौरुषम् ।
मोहादारभ्यते कर्म यत्तत्तामसमुच्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
anubandhaṃ kṣayaṃ hiṃsāmanapekṣya ca pauruṣam
mohādārabhyate karma yattattāmasamucyate
verses.essence: Action undertaken from delusion, without regard for consequence, loss, injury, or one's own capacity — that is called tamasic.
verses.translation: That action which is undertaken from delusion, without a regard for the conseences, loss, injury and (one's own) ability that is declared to be Tamasic (dark).

anvaya.meaning: the consequence, the loss, the injury
from delusion is undertaken the action

verses.explanation: Tamasic action: launched mohāt, out of fog, with four blind spots — what follows from it, what it destroys, whom it wounds, and whether one can even carry it. The reckless venture, the cruelty that never imagined its victim, the commitment made by someone who never measured themselves. Action without a single look ahead, behind, or within.
verses.connection: The action-triad closes — and 18.26 turns the lens from the deed to its doer.
verses.reflection: Before one undertaking today, take the four looks: consequence, cost, harm, and your real capacity.
verses.background: The great threefold catalogue unrolls — the chapter's longest movement: knowledge that sees the one imperishable in all beings, or only separateness, or one fragment as the whole; action obligatory and unattached, or strained by desire, or undertaken blindly; the doer free of ego, or greedy and volatile, or indolent and despairing; then intellect, firmness, and finally happiness itself — poison first and nectar after for sattva, nectar first and poison after for rajas, delusion throughout for tamas. Nothing on earth or in heaven is free of the three strands.

verses.devanagari:
मुक्तसङ्गोऽनहंवादी धृत्युत्साहसमन्वितः ।
सिद्ध्यसिद्ध्योर्निर्विकारः कर्ता सात्त्विक उच्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
muktasaṅgo'nahaṃvādī dhṛtyutsāhasamanvitaḥ
siddhyasiddhyornirvikāraḥ kartā sāttvika ucyate
verses.essence: Free of attachment, without egoism, endowed with steadfastness and zeal, unmoved in success and failure — such an agent is called sattvic.
verses.translation: An agent who is free from attachment, non-egoistic, endowed with firmness and enthusiasm, and unaffected by success or failure, is called Sattvic (pure).

anvaya.meaning: released from attachment, not speaking in 'I'
such an agent is called sattvic

verses.explanation: The sattvic doer, and the portrait corrects a misreading at once: utsāha-samanvitaḥ — *full of zeal*. Detachment is not lukewarmness; this worker burns with energy and holds with nothing. Anahaṁ-vādī, not speaking in 'I': listen to how someone narrates their work and you hear their strand. Nirvikāraḥ when results land either way — the inner weather independent of the scoreboard.
verses.connection: The agent-triad opens: passion and freedom proven compatible in one worker.
verses.reflection: Disprove the stereotype in your own person today: blazing zeal, zero clutch — both at once.
verses.background: The great threefold catalogue unrolls — the chapter's longest movement: knowledge that sees the one imperishable in all beings, or only separateness, or one fragment as the whole; action obligatory and unattached, or strained by desire, or undertaken blindly; the doer free of ego, or greedy and volatile, or indolent and despairing; then intellect, firmness, and finally happiness itself — poison first and nectar after for sattva, nectar first and poison after for rajas, delusion throughout for tamas. Nothing on earth or in heaven is free of the three strands.

verses.devanagari:
रागी कर्मफलप्रेप्सुर्लुब्धो हिंसात्मकोऽशुचिः ।
हर्षशोकान्वितः कर्ता राजसः परिकीर्तितः॥
verses.transliteration:
rāgī karmaphalaprepsurlubdho hiṃsātmako'śuciḥ
harṣaśokānvitaḥ kartā rājasaḥ parikīrtitaḥ
verses.essence: Passionate, hungering for the fruit of action, greedy, violent-natured, impure, swept by joy and sorrow — such an agent is proclaimed rajasic.
verses.translation: Passionate, desiring to obtain the reward of actions, greedy, cruel, impure, moved by joy and sorrow, such an agent is said to be Rajasic (passionate).

anvaya.meaning: accompanied by elation and grief — the agent

verses.explanation: The rajasic doer in six strokes, ending with the signature: harṣa-śokānvitaḥ — *yoked to* elation and grief, riding the result like a small boat rides swells. Every win a high, every setback a wound; the work itself barely tasted between scoreboard checks. Hiṁsātmakaḥ: where wanting is fierce enough, others become obstacles, and obstacles get pushed.
verses.connection: The driven middle: the worker whose mood is outsourced to outcomes.
verses.reflection: Listen for the scoreboard in your mood today — elation and gloom riding results — and step off once.
verses.background: The great threefold catalogue unrolls — the chapter's longest movement: knowledge that sees the one imperishable in all beings, or only separateness, or one fragment as the whole; action obligatory and unattached, or strained by desire, or undertaken blindly; the doer free of ego, or greedy and volatile, or indolent and despairing; then intellect, firmness, and finally happiness itself — poison first and nectar after for sattva, nectar first and poison after for rajas, delusion throughout for tamas. Nothing on earth or in heaven is free of the three strands.

verses.devanagari:
अयुक्तः प्राकृतः स्तब्धः शठो नैष्कृतिकोऽलसः ।
विषादी दीर्घसूत्री च कर्ता तामस उच्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
ayuktaḥ prākṛtaḥ stabdhaḥ śaṭho naiṣkṛtiko'lasaḥ
viṣādī dīrghasūtrī ca kartā tāmasa ucyate
verses.essence: Undisciplined, vulgar, obstinate, deceitful, malicious, lazy, despondent, procrastinating — such an agent is called tamasic.
verses.translation: Unsteady, vulgar, unbending, cheating, malicious, lazy, desponding and procrastinating such an agent is called Tamasic.

anvaya.meaning: such an agent is called tamasic

verses.explanation: The tamasic doer's eight marks include the Gita's most domestic word: dīrgha-sūtrī — 'long-threaded', the one whose every task trails endlessly, never cut and tied; and viṣādī, the chronically sunk. Note where viṣāda began: it was Arjuna's own state in chapter 1, this scripture's starting point — named there as a yoga's first step, named here as a strand to outgrow.
verses.connection: The agent-triad closes — 18.29 turns inward again, to the faculties of judgment and resolve.
verses.reflection: Find your longest-threaded task — the one trailing for months — and cut and tie one end today.

verses.devanagari:
बुद्धेर्भेदं धृतेश्चैव गुणतस्त्रिविधं शृणु ।
प्रोच्यमानमशेषेण पृथक्त्वेन धनञ्जय॥
verses.transliteration:
buddherbhedaṃ dhṛteścaiva guṇatastrividhaṃ śṛṇu
procyamānamaśeṣeṇa pṛthaktvena dhanañjaya
verses.essence: Hear now, Dhananjaya, the threefold division of understanding and of steadfastness, according to the gunas — declared completely and severally.
verses.translation: Hear thou the threefold division of intellect and firmness according to the Gunas, as I declare them fully and distinctly, O Arjuna.

anvaya.meaning: the division of buddhi, and of dhriti

verses.explanation: Two more instruments under the lens: buddhi, the faculty that *discerns* — tells path from precipice — and dhṛti, the deep grip that *holds* a course once chosen. Compass and keel. A life steers by both, so the field guide must grade both: six more portraits, after which the survey turns to happiness itself.
verses.connection: Announces the buddhi and dhriti triads (18.30–35): how we judge, and how we hold.
verses.reflection: Service both instruments today: the compass that discerns, the keel that holds. A life steers by both.

verses.devanagari:
प्रवृत्तिं च निवृत्तिं च कार्याकार्ये भयाभये ।
बन्धं मोक्षं च या वेत्ति बुद्धिः सा पार्थ सात्त्विकी॥
verses.transliteration:
pravṛttiṃ ca nivṛttiṃ ca kāryākārye bhayābhaye
bandhaṃ mokṣaṃ ca yā vetti buddhiḥ sā pārtha sāttvikī
verses.essence: The understanding that knows action and withdrawal, what ought and ought not be done, fear and fearlessness, bondage and liberation — that, Partha, is sattvic.
verses.translation: The intellect which knows the path of work and renunciation, what ought to be done and what ought not to be done, fear and fearlessness, bondage and liberation that intellect is Sattvic (pure), O Arjuna.

anvaya.meaning: what is to be done and not; fear and non-fear
that buddhi, O Partha, is sattvic

verses.explanation: Sattvic understanding holds four distinctions clean: when to act and when to stand back; what duty asks and forbids; what genuinely merits fear and what doesn't; what binds and what frees. Note the modesty of the claim — not omniscience, just an unclouded compass on the questions that steer a life. Most tragedy flows from confusing exactly these pairs.
verses.connection: The clear compass — precisely the discrimination Arjuna lacked at 1.30 and asked for at 2.7.
verses.reflection: Test the four clean distinctions today: act or refrain, ought or ought-not, fear or no, binds or frees.

verses.devanagari:
यया धर्ममधर्मं च कार्यं चाकार्यमेव च ।
अयथावत्प्रजानाति बुद्धिः सा पार्थ राजसी॥
verses.transliteration:
yayā dharmamadharmaṃ ca kāryaṃ cākāryameva ca
ayathāvatprajānāti buddhiḥ sā pārtha rājasī
verses.essence: That by which one knows dharma and adharma, what should and should not be done, incorrectly — that understanding, Partha, is rajasic.
verses.translation: That, by which one wrongly understands Dharma and Adharma and also what ought to be done and what ought not to be done that intellect, O Arjuna, is Rajasic (passionate).

anvaya.meaning: that buddhi, O Partha, is rajasic

verses.explanation: The rajasic compass: ayathāvat — *not as it is*; the needle works but reads a few degrees off, deflected by the magnet of self-interest. Right and wrong are perceived, then quietly redrawn around desire: the convenient becomes the correct. Most rationalization lives here — judgment intact enough to be persuasive, bent enough to mislead.
verses.connection: The deflected middle: discernment in service of the wanting self.
verses.reflection: Watch for the deflected needle today — right and wrong quietly redrawn around what you want.

verses.devanagari:
अधर्मं धर्ममिति या मन्यते तमसावृता ।
सर्वार्थान्विपरीतांश्च बुद्धिः सा पार्थ तामसी॥
verses.transliteration:
adharmaṃ dharmamiti yā manyate tamasāvṛtā
sarvārthānviparītāṃśca buddhiḥ sā pārtha tāmasī
verses.essence: That which, enveloped in darkness, regards adharma as dharma and sees all things reversed — that understanding, Partha, is tamasic.
verses.translation: That, which, enveloped in darkness, sees Adharma as Dharma and all things perverted that intellect, O Arjuna, is Tamasic (dark).

anvaya.meaning: that buddhi, O Partha, is tamasic

verses.explanation: The broken compass: not deflected but inverted — adharmaṁ dharmam iti, wrong worn confidently as right, sarvārthān viparītān, the whole map upside down. The fearful trait of tamasic judgment is its certainty: rajas suspects its own bending; tamas marches, convinced, in precisely the wrong direction. History's catastrophes are mostly this verse at scale.
verses.connection: The inverted compass completes the buddhi-triad; 18.33 turns to what holds a course.
verses.reflection: Beware certainty traveling in exactly the wrong direction — check one conviction's compass today.

verses.devanagari:
धृत्या यया धारयते मनःप्राणेन्द्रियक्रियाः ।
योगेनाव्यभिचारिण्या धृतिः सा पार्थ सात्त्विकी॥
verses.transliteration:
dhṛtyā yayā dhārayate manaḥprāṇendriyakriyāḥ
yogenāvyabhicāriṇyā dhṛtiḥ sā pārtha sāttvikī
verses.essence: The steadfastness by which one holds the activities of mind, breath and senses through unswerving yoga — that steadfastness, Partha, is sattvic.
verses.translation: The unwavering firmness by which, through Yoga, the functions of the mind, the life-force and the senses are restrained that firmness, O Arjuna, is Sattvic (pure).

anvaya.meaning: the steadfastness by which one holds
the activities of mind, breath, senses
that dhriti, O Partha, is sattvic

verses.explanation: Sattvic dhṛti: the keel — a steadiness that holds the whole apparatus, thought, breath and sense, on one heading, avyabhicāriṇyā, without straying. This is the grip that survives dull Tuesdays and broken moods, the force behind every practice still alive after twenty years. Not intensity: constancy — the rarer and stronger of the two.
verses.connection: The holding-power triad opens: resolve in service of the undivided aim.
verses.reflection: Hold one heading all day — thought, breath, and senses on a single course — and call it dhriti.
verses.background: The great threefold catalogue unrolls — the chapter's longest movement: knowledge that sees the one imperishable in all beings, or only separateness, or one fragment as the whole; action obligatory and unattached, or strained by desire, or undertaken blindly; the doer free of ego, or greedy and volatile, or indolent and despairing; then intellect, firmness, and finally happiness itself — poison first and nectar after for sattva, nectar first and poison after for rajas, delusion throughout for tamas. verses.devanagari:
यया तु धर्मकामार्थान्धृत्या धारयतेऽर्जुन ।
प्रसङ्गेन फलाकाङ्क्षी धृतिः सा पार्थ राजसी॥
verses.transliteration:
yayā tu dharmakāmārthāndhṛtyā dhārayate'rjuna
prasaṅgena phalākāṅkṣī dhṛtiḥ sā pārtha rājasī
verses.essence: But the steadfastness by which one holds to duty, pleasure and wealth, craving the fruits of each as occasion offers — that steadfastness, Partha, is rajasic.
verses.translation: But that, O Arjuna, by which, on account of attachment and desire for reward, one holds fast to Dharma (duty), enjoyment of pleasures and earning of wealth that firmness, O Arjuna, is Rajasic (passionate).

anvaya.meaning: but by which — duty, pleasure, wealth
by such firmness one holds, O Arjuna
that dhriti, O Partha, is rajasic

verses.explanation: Rajasic dhṛti is genuinely strong — it powers careers, fortunes, reputations — but its grip serves the three worldly aims with phalākāṅkṣī stamped on each: holding *in order to harvest*. Such steadiness lasts exactly as long as the returns do; the project that stops paying gets dropped, however worthy. Tenacity on commission.
verses.connection: The transactional grip: strong hands, hired by results.
verses.reflection: Notice which commitments you keep only while they pay — tenacity on commission.

verses.devanagari:
यया स्वप्नं भयं शोकं विषादं मदमेव च ।
न विमुञ्चति दुर्मेधा धृतिः सा पार्थ तामसी॥
verses.transliteration:
yayā svapnaṃ bhayaṃ śokaṃ viṣādaṃ madameva ca
na vimuñcati durmedhā dhṛtiḥ sā pārtha tāmasī
verses.essence: That by which a dull person does not let go of sleep, fear, grief, despondency and conceit — that steadfastness, Partha, is tamasic.
verses.translation: That, by which a stupid man does not abandon sleep, fear, grief, despair and also conceit that firmness, O Arjuna, is Tamasic.

anvaya.meaning: that dhriti, O Partha, is tamasic

verses.explanation: The triad's darkest insight: tamas also has a grip — and it is iron. Na vimuñcati, *does not let go*: of oversleep, of fear long after the danger, of grief turned residence, of viṣāda, of self-flattering illusion. The same holding-power that could anchor a practice instead anchors the misery. Tenacity, the verse teaches, is neutral; everything depends on what the fist is closed around.
verses.connection: Completes dhriti: even despair requires a kind of strength — misapplied.
verses.reflection: Find where your strength grips the wrong cargo — fear kept, grief kept, gloom kept — and open one fist.

verses.devanagari:
सुखं त्विदानीं त्रिविधं शृणु मे भरतर्षभ ।
अभ्यासाद्रमते यत्र दुःखान्तं च निगच्छति॥
verses.transliteration:
sukhaṃ tvidānīṃ trividhaṃ śṛṇu me bharatarṣabha
abhyāsādramate yatra duḥkhāntaṃ ca nigacchati
verses.essence: And now hear from me, bull of the Bharatas, the threefold happiness: that in which one comes to delight through practice, and reaches the end of sorrow —
verses.translation: And now hear from Me, O Arjuna, of the threefold pleasure, in which one rejoices by practice and surely comes to the end of pain.

anvaya.meaning: hear from me, O bull of Bharatas
where, through practice, one comes to delight
and reaches the end of sorrow

verses.explanation: The survey's final and most intimate subject: sukha, happiness itself — for every strand sells its own brand, and choosing among them is the whole art of a life. The sattvic kind is introduced by two credentials: abhyāsāt ramate, it must be *practiced into* — no instant delivery — and it actually ends sorrow rather than postponing it.
verses.connection: Opens the happiness-triad (18.36–39): the deepest diagnostic of all — what does your joy cost, and when?
verses.reflection: Ask of today's happiness the master question: what does this joy cost, and when is the bill due?

verses.devanagari:
यत्तदग्रे विषमिव परिणामेऽमृतोपमम् ।
तत्सुखं सात्त्विकं प्रोक्तमात्मबुद्धिप्रसादजम्॥
verses.transliteration:
yattadagre viṣamiva pariṇāme'mṛtopamam
tatsukhaṃ sāttvikaṃ proktamātmabuddhiprasādajam
verses.essence: — that which is like poison at first but like nectar in the end, born of the serene clarity of self-understanding: that happiness is declared sattvic.
verses.translation: That which is like poison at first but in the end like nectar that happiness is declared to be Sattvic, born of the purity of one's own mind due to Self-realisation.

anvaya.meaning: that which at first is like poison
born of the clarity of self-understanding

verses.explanation: The signature of real happiness: agre viṣam iva — poison first. The early morning practice, the honest conversation, the discipline's first cold weeks: all bitter at the threshold, amṛtopamam — nectar — in the ripening. Its source is given precisely: ātma-buddhi-prasāda-jam, born of the settling-clear of the mind in the self, joy as clarity's by-product, not stimulation's. Yat tad agre visham iva — poison first, nectar in the ripening: the taste-curve of real happiness, born — the commentaries underline the source — atma-buddhi-prasada-jam, from the settling-clear of the understanding in the self. The teachers read the verse as the Gita's test for any joy on offer: plot its curve. Whatever charges admission in discomfort and pays in clarity is sattvic; the reverse curve (18.38) is the world's usual merchandise.
verses.connection: The first taste-curve: bitter-then-sweet — the exact inverse of what 18.38 describes.
verses.reflection: Choose one poison-first pleasure today — the practice, the honest talk — and trust the nectar at the end.

Nothing on earth or in heaven is free of the three strands.

verses.devanagari:
विषयेन्द्रियसंयोगाद्यत्तदग्रेऽमृतोपमम् ।
परिणामे विषमिव तत्सुखं राजसं स्मृतम्॥
verses.transliteration:
viṣayendriyasaṃyogādyattadagre'mṛtopamam
pariṇāme viṣamiva tatsukhaṃ rājasaṃ smṛtam
verses.essence: That which arises from the contact of senses with their objects — like nectar at first but like poison in the end: that happiness is held to be rajasic.
verses.translation: That happiness which arises from the contact of the sense-organs with the objects, which is at first like nectar, and in the end like poison that is declared to be Rajasic.

anvaya.meaning: from the union of senses and objects
that which at first is nectar-like

verses.explanation: The same curve, reversed: nectar at the first sip, poison in the sediment — the indulgence, the conquest, the thrill, each sweet exactly while the senses touch their object and souring as the contact fades into craving, depletion, consequence. Neither curve is moralized; they are simply *plotted*, and the reader is left holding the graph of their own evenings.
verses.connection: The mirrored taste-curve: pleasure that invoices later — most of what the market calls happiness.
verses.reflection: Watch one nectar-first pleasure turn today — sweet at contact, souring in the sediment — just watch.

verses.devanagari:
यदग्रे चानुबन्धे च सुखं मोहनमात्मनः ।
निद्रालस्यप्रमादोत्थं तत्तामसमुदाहृतम्॥
verses.transliteration:
yadagre cānubandhe ca sukhaṃ mohanamātmanaḥ
nidrālasyapramādotthaṃ tattāmasamudāhṛtam
verses.essence: That happiness which deludes the self both at the start and in the sequel — arising from sleep, sloth and heedlessness — is declared tamasic.
verses.translation: That happiness which at first as well as in the seel deludes the self, and which arises from sleep, indolence and heedlessness that is declared to be Tamasic.

anvaya.meaning: which at the start and in the sequel
is a happiness that deludes the self

verses.explanation: The third curve never rises: mohanam ātmanaḥ at both ends — a comfort that fogs going in and fogs coming out. The oversleep, the numbing scroll, the haze chosen *because* it is haze: not pleasure exactly, but anesthesia mistaken for rest. Its three springs — sleep beyond need, sloth, heedlessness — are tamas's own roster from 14.8, now sold as happiness.
verses.connection: The flat-line comfort completes the triad; 18.40 closes the whole survey with a universal law.
verses.reflection: Name the flat-line comfort honestly once: the haze chosen because it is haze. Anesthesia is not rest.
verses.background: The great threefold catalogue unrolls — the chapter's longest movement: knowledge that sees the one imperishable in all beings, or only separateness, or one fragment as the whole; action obligatory and unattached, or strained by desire, or undertaken blindly; the doer free of ego, or greedy and volatile, or indolent and despairing; then intellect, firmness, and finally happiness itself — poison first and nectar after for sattva, nectar first and poison after for rajas, delusion throughout for tamas. Nothing on earth or in heaven is free of the three strands.

verses.devanagari:
न तदस्ति पृथिव्यां वा दिवि देवेषु वा पुनः ।
सत्त्वं प्रकृतिजैर्मुक्तं यदेभिः स्यात्त्रिभिर्गुणैः॥
verses.transliteration:
na tadasti pṛthivyāṃ vā divi deveṣu vā punaḥ
sattvaṃ prakṛtijairmuktaṃ yadebhiḥ syāttribhirguṇaiḥ
verses.essence: There is no being, whether on earth or again among the gods in heaven, that is free from these three gunas born of prakriti.
verses.translation: There is no being on earth or again in heaven among the gods, that is liberated from the three alities born of Nature.

anvaya.meaning: there is nothing, whether on earth
or again in heaven among the gods
no being released from the prakriti-born

verses.explanation: The survey seals itself with a totality clause: nothing anywhere — no creature, no king, no *god* — stands outside the three strands; the whole ladder of existence, bottom to shining top, is woven of them. The field guide is therefore universal, and so is its implication: within nature there is no exit, only better weather. The exit lies elsewhere — and the chapter now turns to find it through one's own born work.
verses.connection: Closes the guna-survey absolutely — pivoting to 18.41: if no one escapes the strands, each must work through their own.
verses.reflection: Stop seeking strand-free people today — none exist, on earth or in heaven. Seek better weather instead.
verses.background: The great threefold catalogue unrolls — the chapter's longest movement: knowledge that sees the one imperishable in all beings, or only separateness, or one fragment as the whole; action obligatory and unattached, or strained by desire, or undertaken blindly; the doer free of ego, or greedy and volatile, or indolent and despairing; then intellect, firmness, and finally happiness itself — poison first and nectar after for sattva, nectar first and poison after for rajas, delusion throughout for tamas. Nothing on earth or in heaven is free of the three strands.

verses.devanagari:
ब्राह्मणक्षत्रियविशां शूद्राणां च परन्तप ।
कर्माणि प्रविभक्तानि स्वभावप्रभवैर्गुणैः॥
verses.transliteration:
brāhmaṇakṣatriyaviśāṃ śūdrāṇāṃ ca parantapa
karmāṇi pravibhaktāni svabhāvaprabhavairguṇaiḥ
verses.essence: The duties of brahmanas, kshatriyas, vaishyas and shudras, Parantapa, are distributed according to the qualities born of their own natures.
verses.translation: Of Brahmanas, Kshatriyas and Vaisyas, as also of Sudras, O Arjuna, the duties are distributed according to the alities born of their own nature.

anvaya.meaning: by the gunas born of inner nature

verses.explanation: The chapter turns from analysis to vocation, and its stated basis matters: svabhāva-prabhavaiḥ guṇaiḥ — work apportioned by the *qualities arising from one's own nature*, the guna-composition just surveyed in 18.19–40. The Gita grounds calling in temperament, not pedigree: the question it asks of a person is what their nature is made of, and what work that nature can pour itself into.
verses.connection: Applies the completed guna-science to vocation — read with 4.13, where the same division is tied to guna and karma.
verses.schools_note: The four works 'apportioned by gunas born of inner nature' is the tradition's contested ground: classical commentators read svabhava through birth and past karma together; the reformers' line — Vivekananda, Gandhi, Aurobindo — pressed the verse's actual basis (guna and svabhava, with birth unmentioned here) toward vocation by temperament. The verse's own center holds for all: work is dharma when it expresses what one is.
verses.reflection: Read your own composition today — what qualities actually arise from your nature — and assign work accordingly.
verses.background: The teaching turns to vocation: the works of brahmana, kshatriya, vaishya, and shudra are apportioned by the qualities born of inner nature — serenity and knowledge; heroism and lordly generosity; agriculture, cattle, trade; and service. Devoted each to his own work, a person attains perfection — worshiping, through his own duty, Him from whom all beings arise. Better one's own dharma though flawed; the work born with you, though faulty, should not be abandoned, for all undertakings are veiled by fault as fire by smoke.

verses.devanagari:
शमो दमस्तपः शौचं क्षान्तिरार्जवमेव च ।
ज्ञानं विज्ञानमास्तिक्यं ब्रह्मकर्म स्वभावजम्॥
verses.transliteration:
śamo damastapaḥ śaucaṃ kṣāntirārjavameva ca
jñānaṃ vijñānamāstikyaṃ brahmakarma svabhāvajam
verses.essence: Serenity, restraint, austerity, purity, forbearance, uprightness, knowledge, realization, and faith in the beyond — these are the works of a brahmana, born of inner nature.
verses.translation: Serenity, self-restraint, austerity, purity, forgiveness and also uprightness, knowledge, realisation and belief in God are the duties of the Brahmanas, born of (their own) nature.
verses.explanation: The contemplative nature described by its outputs: inner calm and outer restraint, the scrubbed life, patience, straightness, learning ripened into vijñāna — lived realization — and āstikya, the settled trust that reality has a depth. Note that everything listed is a quality, not a privilege: this 'work' is a way of being that teaches by existing.
verses.connection: First of the four nature-portraits (18.42–44): the temperament whose work is clarity itself.
verses.reflection: Borrow one brahmana-quality for the day: calm, restraint, or the settled trust that reality has depth.
verses.background: The teaching turns to vocation: the works of brahmana, kshatriya, vaishya, and shudra are apportioned by the qualities born of inner nature — serenity and knowledge; heroism and lordly generosity; agriculture, cattle, trade; and service. verses.devanagari:
शौर्यं तेजो धृतिर्दाक्ष्यं युद्धे चाप्यपलायनम् ।
दानमीश्वरभावश्च क्षात्रं कर्म स्वभावजम्॥
verses.transliteration:
śauryaṃ tejo dhṛtirdākṣyaṃ yuddhe cāpyapalāyanam
dānamīśvarabhāvaśca kṣātraṃ karma svabhāvajam
verses.essence: Heroism, fire, fortitude, skill, refusal to flee in battle, generosity and lordliness — these are the works of a kshatriya, born of inner nature.
verses.translation: Prowess, splendour, firmness, dexterity and also not fleeing from battle, generosity and lordliness are the duties of the Kshatriyas, born of (their own) nature.
verses.explanation: The guardian nature: courage with tejas behind it, the grip of dhṛti, dākṣya — competence under pressure — and the mark Arjuna cannot miss hearing: yuddhe cāpy apalāyanam, *not fleeing in battle*. Paired, crucially, with dāna and īśvara-bhāva: power that protects and gives, or it is not this dharma at all. The portrait is a mirror held up to the listener.
verses.connection: Arjuna's own nature read back to him — the verse beneath Krishna's entire case since 2.31.
verses.reflection: Borrow one kshatriya-quality too: stand somewhere today where retreating would be easier.
verses.background: The teaching turns to vocation: the works of brahmana, kshatriya, vaishya, and shudra are apportioned by the qualities born of inner nature — serenity and knowledge; heroism and lordly generosity; agriculture, cattle, trade; and service. Better one's own dharma though flawed; the work born with you, though faulty, should not be abandoned, for all undertakings are veiled by fault as fire by smoke.

verses.devanagari:
कृषिगौरक्ष्यवाणिज्यं वैश्यकर्म स्वभावजम् ।
परिचर्यात्मकं कर्म शूद्रस्यापि स्वभावजम्॥
verses.transliteration:
kṛṣigaurakṣyavāṇijyaṃ vaiśyakarma svabhāvajam
paricaryātmakaṃ karma śūdrasyāpi svabhāvajam
verses.essence: Agriculture, cattle-keeping and trade are the works of a vaishya, born of inner nature; and work of service is the work of a shudra, born of inner nature.
verses.translation: Agriculture, cattle-rearing and trade are the duties of the Vaisya (merchant), born of (their own) nature; and action consisting of service is the duty of the Sudra (servant-class), born of (their own) nature.

anvaya.meaning: of the shudra too, born of nature

verses.explanation: The remaining two portraits: the productive nature — growing, tending, trading, the arts that feed and circulate a world — and the nature whose gift is paricaryā, direct service and support. The refrain svabhāvajam sounds a fourth time, insisting on its frame: these are descriptions of temperament finding its channel. And the verses that follow immediately level the field — what matters is not *which* work, but what one does with one's own.
verses.connection: Completes the four portraits — so that 18.45–46 can declare them equal at the summit.
verses.reflection: Honor the producers and the servers in your world today — feeding and supporting are sacred grammar.
verses.background: The teaching turns to vocation: the works of brahmana, kshatriya, vaishya, and shudra are apportioned by the qualities born of inner nature — serenity and knowledge; heroism and lordly generosity; agriculture, cattle, trade; and service. verses.devanagari:
स्वे स्वे कर्मण्यभिरतः संसिद्धिं लभते नरः ।
स्वकर्मनिरतः सिद्धिं यथा विन्दति तच्छृणु॥
verses.transliteration:
sve sve karmaṇyabhirataḥ saṃsiddhiṃ labhate naraḥ
svakarmanirataḥ siddhiṃ yathā vindati tacchṛṇu
verses.essence: Devoted each to their own work, a person attains perfection. Hear now how one devoted to one's own work finds that perfection.
verses.translation: He who does actions, offering them to Brahman, and abandoning attachment, is not tainted by sin, just as a lotus-leaf is not tainted by water.

anvaya.meaning: in each one's own work, delighting
devoted to one's own work, perfection

verses.explanation: The leveling verse: sve sve karmaṇi — in *each their own* work — saṁsiddhim, the full perfection, is attainable. Not perfection despite one's station but through it; the door is wherever you are standing. Abhirataḥ, *delighting* in it: the Gita asks not for resignation to one's work but for a wholeheartedness that turns the work transparent.
verses.connection: Universalizes the four portraits: every authentic vocation reaches the same summit — 18.46 gives the mechanism.
verses.reflection: Delight in your own work once today — abhirata — perfection enters through the door you already stand at.
verses.background: The teaching turns to vocation: the works of brahmana, kshatriya, vaishya, and shudra are apportioned by the qualities born of inner nature — serenity and knowledge; heroism and lordly generosity; agriculture, cattle, trade; and service. Better one's own dharma though flawed; the work born with you, though faulty, should not be abandoned, for all undertakings are veiled by fault as fire by smoke.

verses.devanagari:
यतः प्रवृत्तिर्भूतानां येन सर्वमिदं ततम् ।
स्वकर्मणा तमभ्यर्च्य सिद्धिं विन्दति मानवः॥
verses.transliteration:
yataḥ pravṛttirbhūtānāṃ yena sarvamidaṃ tatam
svakarmaṇā tamabhyarcya siddhiṃ vindati mānavaḥ
verses.essence: Worshiping, through one's own work, the One from whom all beings arise and by whom all this is pervaded — a person finds perfection.
verses.translation: He from Whom all the beings have evolved and by Whom all this is pervaded worshipping Him with his own duty, man attains perfection.

anvaya.meaning: from whom is the activity of beings
by whom all this is pervaded
worshiping Him by one's own work

verses.explanation: The mechanism, and one of the Gita's supreme verses: sva-karmaṇā tam abhyarcya — worship Him *with your own work*. The deed itself becomes the offering: the field ploughed, the ledger kept, the patient tended, the line of code — each laid on the altar of the One who pervades all this. No change of occupation required, only of address. Every workplace, so addressed, is a temple mid-liturgy. Yatah pravrittir bhutanam — worship Him from whom all beings arise, with your own work as the offering: sva-karmana tam abhyarchya. The commentaries call this the Gita's charter for the householder's liberation: Ramanuja reads abhyarchya literally — duty as arcana, flowers on the altar; Shankara as the purification that ripens into knowledge. Either way the revolution stands: siddhim vindati manavah — perfection found not beside one's livelihood but through it.
verses.connection: The summit of the vocation-teaching: karma-yoga and bhakti fused into a single gesture.
verses.schools_note: Commentators treat this as the Gita's charter for lay spirituality. Ramanuja reads work-as-worship as literal arcana — daily duty offered like flowers; Shankara as purification that ripens into knowledge. Either way the radical claim stands: the ordinary livelihood, rightly addressed, is a complete spiritual path.
verses.reflection: Make your livelihood the liturgy once today: offer the actual task, as worship, to the All-Pervading.

verses.devanagari:
श्रेयान्स्वधर्मो विगुणः परधर्मात्स्वनुष्ठितात् ।
स्वभावनियतं कर्म कुर्वन्नाप्नोति किल्बिषम्॥
verses.transliteration:
śreyānsvadharmo viguṇaḥ paradharmātsvanuṣṭhitāt
svabhāvaniyataṃ karma kurvannāpnoti kilbiṣam
verses.essence: Better one's own dharma, though imperfect, than another's dharma well performed. Doing the work ordained by one's own nature, one incurs no fault.
verses.translation: Better is one's own duty (though) destitute of merits, than the duty of another well performed. He who does the duty ordained by his own nature incurs no sin.

anvaya.meaning: better one's own dharma, though flawed
the work fixed by one's own nature

verses.explanation: The maxim of 3.35 returns for the summation, with its ground now visible: svabhāva-niyatam — the work *your nature itself assigns*. Borrowed callings, however gleaming, run against the grain of what you are; your own, even viguṇa — rough, flawed, unglamorous — runs with it, and so purifies rather than stains. Authenticity outranks polish; the fault lies not in imperfect work but in abandoned selfhood.
verses.connection: 3.35 restated at the summit — now backed by the full svabhava-doctrine of 18.41–46.
verses.reflection: Choose your rough-grained calling over a borrowed gleaming one, again, today — and incur no stain.
verses.background: The teaching turns to vocation: the works of brahmana, kshatriya, vaishya, and shudra are apportioned by the qualities born of inner nature — serenity and knowledge; heroism and lordly generosity; agriculture, cattle, trade; and service. verses.devanagari:
सहजं कर्म कौन्तेय सदोषमपि न त्यजेत् ।
सर्वारम्भा हि दोषेण धूमेनाग्निरिवावृताः॥
verses.transliteration:
sahajaṃ karma kaunteya sadoṣamapi na tyajet
sarvārambhā hi doṣeṇa dhūmenāgnirivāvṛtāḥ
verses.essence: One should not abandon the work born of one's nature, Kaunteya, though it carry a flaw — for all undertakings are veiled by some fault, as fire by smoke.
verses.translation: One should not abandon, O Arjuna, the duty to which one is born, though faulty; for, all undertakings are enveloped by evil, as fire by smoke.

anvaya.meaning: though with fault, one should not abandon
for all undertakings, by some fault
are veiled — as fire by smoke

verses.explanation: The verse that disarms perfectionism forever: sarvārambhā hi doṣeṇa — *every* undertaking, without exception, comes wrapped in some flaw, dhūmenāgnir iva, as fire comes wrapped in smoke. Waiting for stainless work is waiting for smokeless fire. The instruction: keep the sahaja karma, the work you were born carrying, and let it burn — smoke included. Saha-jam karma kaunteya — the work born with you, do not abandon though flawed: sarvarambha hi doshena dhumenagnir ivavritah — all undertakings are wrapped in fault as fire in smoke. The commentaries treasure the image as the end of perfectionism: the smoke is not evidence against the fire. Jnaneshwar adds the corollary: tend your own hearth's smoke rather than admiring the apparent smokelessness of a neighbor's distant flame.
verses.connection: Answers the purist's last objection — and frees Arjuna from demanding a war without smoke.
verses.reflection: Expect smoke with every fire today — keep the born work, flaws included; smokeless fire does not exist.
verses.background: The teaching turns to vocation: the works of brahmana, kshatriya, vaishya, and shudra are apportioned by the qualities born of inner nature — serenity and knowledge; heroism and lordly generosity; agriculture, cattle, trade; and service.
