Better one's own dharma though flawed; the work born with you, though faulty, should not be abandoned, for all undertakings are veiled by fault as fire by smoke.

verses.devanagari:
असक्तबुद्धिः सर्वत्र जितात्मा विगतस्पृहः ।
नैष्कर्म्यसिद्धिं परमां संन्यासेनाधिगच्छति॥
verses.transliteration:
asaktabuddhiḥ sarvatra jitātmā vigataspṛhaḥ
naiṣkarmyasiddhiṃ paramāṃ saṃnyāsenādhigacchati
verses.essence: With understanding unattached everywhere, self conquered, longing departed — by renunciation one attains the supreme perfection of freedom from action.
verses.translation: He whose intellect is unattached everywhere, who has subdued his self, from whom desire has fled, he by renunciation, attains the supreme state of freedom from action.
verses.explanation: The paradoxical prize: naiṣkarmya-siddhi, the perfection of *non-action* — attained, the whole chapter has argued, not by stopping work but by the saṁnyāsa that empties it of clutching: intellect unhooked, self governed, spṛhā gone. One still acts; nothing any longer *acts back* upon one. Stillness achieved at full speed.
verses.connection: Names the goal of 18.45–48's path — and 18.50 begins describing the final ascent to Brahman.
verses.reflection: Taste actionlessness at full speed once: act completely, with nothing in you grasping the act.
verses.background: The final ascent is mapped: unattached understanding, self conquered, longing gone — by renunciation to the supreme perfection of actionlessness; then, in brief, the climb to Brahman: purified intellect, solitude, light food, governed speech and body, constant meditation, dispassion; egoism, force, arrogance, desire, anger, possession released — serene, become Brahman, one grieves not, craves not, equal to all beings — and attains supreme devotion to Me, by which alone I am known in truth, and entered.

verses.devanagari:
सिद्धिं प्राप्तो यथा ब्रह्म तथाप्नोति निबोध मे ।
समासेनैव कौन्तेय निष्ठा ज्ञानस्य या परा॥
verses.transliteration:
siddhiṃ prāpto yathā brahma tathāpnoti nibodha me
samāsenaiva kaunteya niṣṭhā jñānasya yā parā
verses.essence: Learn from me in brief, Kaunteya, how one who has attained perfection reaches Brahman — that supreme consummation of knowledge.
verses.translation: Learn from Me in brief, O Arjuna, how he who has attained perfection reaches Brahman (the Eternal), that supreme state of knowledge.

anvaya.meaning: which is the supreme consummation of knowledge

verses.explanation: The final ascent announced: from siddhi — the ripeness won through consecrated work — to Brahman itself, the parā niṣṭhā, knowledge's last standing-place. What follows in 18.51–53 is the Gita's closing portrait of contemplative consummation: the ladder's top rungs, drawn samāsena, in spare, swift strokes.
verses.connection: Opens the three-verse ascent (18.51–53): from perfected action into the silence of Brahman.
verses.reflection: Set tonight's aim at the consummation: not better performance, but knowledge's final resting place.
verses.background: The final ascent is mapped: unattached understanding, self conquered, longing gone — by renunciation to the supreme perfection of actionlessness; then, in brief, the climb to Brahman: purified intellect, solitude, light food, governed speech and body, constant meditation, dispassion; egoism, force, arrogance, desire, anger, possession released — serene, become Brahman, one grieves not, craves not, equal to all beings — and attains supreme devotion to Me, by which alone I am known in truth, and entered.

verses.devanagari:
बुद्ध्या विशुद्धया युक्तो धृत्यात्मानं नियम्य च ।
शब्दादीन्विषयांस्त्यक्त्वा रागद्वेषौ व्युदस्य च॥
verses.transliteration:
buddhyā viśuddhayā yukto dhṛtyātmānaṃ niyamya ca
śabdādīnviṣayāṃstyaktvā rāgadveṣau vyudasya ca
verses.essence: Endowed with a purified understanding, restraining the self with firmness, relinquishing sound and the other sense-objects, casting aside passion and hatred —
verses.translation: Endowed with a pure intellect, controlling the self by firmness, relinishing sound and other objects and abandoning attraction and hatred.

anvaya.meaning: and restraining the self with steadfastness
relinquishing sound and the other objects
and casting off passion and aversion

verses.explanation: The ascent's first stage gathers the chapter's own instruments: the buddhi of 18.30, now viśuddhā, scrubbed clear; the sattvic dhṛti of 18.33 as the holding force; the senses gently unplugged from their objects; and the two old magnets, rāga and dveṣa, set down at last. The climber travels light.
verses.connection: Stage one of the ascent: the inner equipment, purified and stilled.
verses.reflection: Travel light into one task today: judgment cleaned, grip steady, the two old magnets set down.
verses.background: The final ascent is mapped: unattached understanding, self conquered, longing gone — by renunciation to the supreme perfection of actionlessness; then, in brief, the climb to Brahman: purified intellect, solitude, light food, governed speech and body, constant meditation, dispassion; egoism, force, arrogance, desire, anger, possession released — serene, become Brahman, one grieves not, craves not, equal to all beings — and attains supreme devotion to Me, by which alone I am known in truth, and entered.

verses.devanagari:
विविक्तसेवी लघ्वाशी यतवाक्कायमानसः ।
ध्यानयोगपरो नित्यं वैराग्यं समुपाश्रितः॥
verses.transliteration:
viviktasevī laghvāśī yatavākkāyamānasaḥ
dhyānayogaparo nityaṃ vairāgyaṃ samupāśritaḥ
verses.essence: — dwelling in solitude, eating lightly, with speech, body and mind governed, ever absorbed in the yoga of meditation, taking firm refuge in dispassion —
verses.translation: Dwelling in solitude, eating but little, with speech, body and mind subdued, always engaged in meditation and concentration, resorting to dispassion.

anvaya.meaning: ever intent on the yoga of meditation

verses.explanation: Stage two: the outer life pared to match the inner — solitude sought, the plate light, the three instruments of expression (voice, body, mind) under one governance, meditation no longer an appointment but the day's axis. Vairāgyaṁ samupāśritaḥ: dispassion not as deprivation but as *refuge* — the place one finally gets to rest.
verses.connection: Stage two: the simplified life of ch6's meditator, returning as the path's last mile.
verses.reflection: Simplify one outer thing today to match an inner intention — lighter plate, quieter hour, governed word.
verses.background: The final ascent is mapped: unattached understanding, self conquered, longing gone — by renunciation to the supreme perfection of actionlessness; then, in brief, the climb to Brahman: purified intellect, solitude, light food, governed speech and body, constant meditation, dispassion; egoism, force, arrogance, desire, anger, possession released — serene, become Brahman, one grieves not, craves not, equal to all beings — and attains supreme devotion to Me, by which alone I am known in truth, and entered.

verses.devanagari:
अहंकारं बलं दर्पं कामं क्रोधं परिग्रहम् ।
विमुच्य निर्ममः शान्तो ब्रह्मभूयाय कल्पते॥
verses.transliteration:
ahaṃkāraṃ balaṃ darpaṃ kāmaṃ krodhaṃ parigraham
vimucya nirmamaḥ śānto brahmabhūyāya kalpate
verses.essence: — having abandoned egoism, force, arrogance, desire, anger and possession; free of 'mine', tranquil — one is fit for becoming Brahman.
verses.translation: Having abandoned egoism, strength, arrogance, desire, anger and covetousness, and free from the notion of 'mine' and peaceful, he is fit for becoming Brahman.

anvaya.meaning: one is fit for becoming Brahman

verses.explanation: The last things set down — and they are the heavy ones: the I-knot itself, the will to force, the swagger, the old pair desire-and-anger, and parigraha, the instinct to accumulate and hold. What remains when all six are released is described in two words: nirmamaḥ śāntaḥ — nothing called mine, and peace. Such a one is kalpate, *fit*: Brahman-becoming is less an achievement than a fitness finally attained.
verses.connection: The ascent completes: emptied of the six weights, the climber and the summit are no longer two — 18.54 describes the view.
verses.reflection: Set down the heavy six where you find them today: I-knot, force, swagger, craving, anger, hoarding.
verses.background: The final ascent is mapped: unattached understanding, self conquered, longing gone — by renunciation to the supreme perfection of actionlessness; then, in brief, the climb to Brahman: purified intellect, solitude, light food, governed speech and body, constant meditation, dispassion; egoism, force, arrogance, desire, anger, possession released — serene, become Brahman, one grieves not, craves not, equal to all beings — and attains supreme devotion to Me, by which alone I am known in truth, and entered.

verses.devanagari:
ब्रह्मभूतः प्रसन्नात्मा न शोचति न काङ्क्षति ।
समः सर्वेषु भूतेषु मद्भक्तिं लभते पराम्॥
verses.transliteration:
brahmabhūtaḥ prasannātmā na śocati na kāṅkṣati
samaḥ sarveṣu bhūteṣu madbhaktiṃ labhate parām
verses.essence: Having become Brahman, serene in self, one neither grieves nor desires; equal toward all beings, one attains supreme devotion to me.
verses.translation: Becoming Brahman, serene in the Self, he neither grieves nor desires, the same to all beings, he obtains supreme devotion to Me.

anvaya.meaning: one attains supreme devotion to me

verses.explanation: The Gita's great surprise, placed at the very summit: brahma-bhūtaḥ — having *become* Brahman, beyond grief and craving, equal to all — what does such a one attain? Mad-bhaktiṁ parām: supreme *love*. Devotion is not the ladder discarded at the top; it is what the top opens into. Union does not end the relationship; it perfects it — the liberated do not stop loving God; they finally begin. Brahma-bhutah prasannatma — having become Brahman, serene, beyond grief and craving, equal to all — mad-bhaktim labhate param: he attains supreme devotion to Me. The verse the bhakti schools hold as their crown jewel: love stands above liberation, the fruit the freed tree finally bears. Shankara reads para bhakti here as knowledge in its fullness; Ramanuja and the Gaudiyas as the soul's eternal vocation beginning. All agree on the shock of the sequence: union is not the end of the relationship but its perfection.
verses.connection: The summit's disclosure: jnana flowers into bhakti — inverting every assumption that knowledge outgrows love.
verses.reflection: Watch grief and craving both go quiet for one moment — and notice love rising in the cleared space.
verses.background: The final ascent is mapped: unattached understanding, self conquered, longing gone — by renunciation to the supreme perfection of actionlessness; then, in brief, the climb to Brahman: purified intellect, solitude, light food, governed speech and body, constant meditation, dispassion; egoism, force, arrogance, desire, anger, possession released — serene, become Brahman, one grieves not, craves not, equal to all beings — and attains supreme devotion to Me, by which alone I am known in truth, and entered.

verses.devanagari:
भक्त्या मामभिजानाति यावान्यश्चास्मि तत्त्वतः ।
ततो मां तत्त्वतो ज्ञात्वा विशते तदनन्तरम्॥
verses.transliteration:
bhaktyā māmabhijānāti yāvānyaścāsmi tattvataḥ
tato māṃ tattvato jñātvā viśate tadanantaram
verses.essence: By devotion one knows me in truth — how great I am and who I am. Then, knowing me in truth, one enters into me forthwith.
verses.translation: By devotion he knows Me in truth, what and who I am; then having known Me in truth, he forthwith enters into the Supreme.

anvaya.meaning: by devotion one fully knows me
how great, and who I am, in truth

verses.explanation: The epistemology of love, completing 18.54: bhaktyā mām abhijānāti — only devotion knows him *tattvataḥ*, as he actually is, in both extent (yāvān) and identity (yaḥ). Analysis circles the fortress; love is handed the key. And then the verb held back since 11.54: viśate — one *enters*. Knower into Known, lover into Beloved: the Gita's final account of liberation is an entering.
verses.connection: Twins with 11.54: love as the one instrument that knows God whole — and the door through which one passes in.
verses.schools_note: 'Bhaktya mam abhijanati' — by devotion one knows Me in truth, then enters: Ramanuja's school reads the sequence as para-bhakti ripening into parama-bhakti, communion as the soul's final estate; Shankara reads the entering as the dissolution of separateness in knowledge-become-love. The verse is both schools' favorite proof that the Gita's epistemology ends in intimacy: tattvatah — 'as I really am' — is disclosed only to love.
verses.reflection: Let love be your method of knowing once today — study someone or something by cherishing it.
verses.background: The final ascent is mapped: unattached understanding, self conquered, longing gone — by renunciation to the supreme perfection of actionlessness; then, in brief, the climb to Brahman: purified intellect, solitude, light food, governed speech and body, constant meditation, dispassion; egoism, force, arrogance, desire, anger, possession released — serene, become Brahman, one grieves not, craves not, equal to all beings — and attains supreme devotion to Me, by which alone I am known in truth, and entered.

verses.devanagari:
सर्वकर्माण्यपि सदा कुर्वाणो मद्व्यपाश्रयः ।
मत्प्रसादादवाप्नोति शाश्वतं पदमव्ययम्॥
verses.transliteration:
sarvakarmāṇyapi sadā kurvāṇo madvyapāśrayaḥ
matprasādādavāpnoti śāśvataṃ padamavyayam
verses.essence: Even while performing all actions, one who takes refuge in me attains, by my grace, the eternal imperishable abode.
verses.translation: Doing all actions always having taken refuge in Me, by My grace he obtains the eternal indestructible state of being.
verses.explanation: The teaching descends from the summit back to the street, deliberately: sarva-karmāṇy api sadā kurvāṇaḥ — doing *everything, always* — the full working life, untrimmed. The two operative words: mad-vyapāśrayaḥ, refuge taken in him, and mat-prasādāt, *by my grace*. Effort builds the readiness; grace closes the distance. The eternal abode, reached without ever leaving one's post.
verses.connection: Reassures the householder after the renunciate's ascent: the same destination, reached amid all duties, by refuge and grace.
verses.reflection: Keep every duty and take refuge anyway — the eternal abode is reached without leaving your post.
verses.background: The teaching narrows to its personal close: doing all actions always, taking refuge in Me, by My grace one gains the eternal abode — so mentally resign all acts to Me and be ever-minded of Me: you shall cross every difficulty by My grace; but if from egoism you will not listen, you shall perish. Your own nature will compel what delusion now refuses; the Lord dwells in the heart of all beings, whirling them as if mounted on a machine. To Him surrender with your whole being. Thus the knowledge more secret than all secrets has been declared: reflect on it fully — then act as you choose.

verses.devanagari:
चेतसा सर्वकर्माणि मयि संन्यस्य मत्परः ।
बुद्धियोगमुपाश्रित्य मच्चित्तः सततं भव॥
verses.transliteration:
cetasā sarvakarmāṇi mayi saṃnyasya matparaḥ
buddhiyogamupāśritya maccittaḥ satataṃ bhava
verses.essence: Mentally renouncing all actions in me, holding me as the highest, resorting to the yoga of understanding — be ever-minded of me.
verses.translation: Mentally renouncing all actions in Me, having Me as the highest goal, resorting to the Yoga of discrimination do thou ever fix thy mind on Me.

anvaya.meaning: renouncing in me, holding me supreme
resorting to the yoga of buddhi
be ever of mind absorbed in me

verses.explanation: The whole path, issued as one personal instruction: cetasā — *with the mind* — deposit every action in him as it happens; keep him as the pole-star (mat-paraḥ); steady it all with buddhi-yoga, the disciplined understanding of chapter 2; and the summary imperative: mac-cittaḥ satataṁ bhava — be, continuously, one whose thought rests in me. Four moves, one life.
verses.connection: The teaching turns imperative and intimate — the prescription whose promise and warning follow in 18.58.
verses.reflection: Run the four moves through one hour: deposit acts in Him, face Him, steady the buddhi, remember.
verses.background: The teaching narrows to its personal close: doing all actions always, taking refuge in Me, by My grace one gains the eternal abode — so mentally resign all acts to Me and be ever-minded of Me: you shall cross every difficulty by My grace; but if from egoism you will not listen, you shall perish. Your own nature will compel what delusion now refuses; the Lord dwells in the heart of all beings, whirling them as if mounted on a machine. To Him surrender with your whole being. Thus the knowledge more secret than all secrets has been declared: reflect on it fully — then act as you choose.

verses.devanagari:
मच्चित्तः सर्वदुर्गाणि मत्प्रसादात्तरिष्यसि ।
अथ चेत्त्वमहंकारान्न श्रोष्यसि विनङ्क्ष्यसि॥
verses.transliteration:
maccittaḥ sarvadurgāṇi matprasādāttariṣyasi
atha cettvamahaṃkārānna śroṣyasi vinaṅkṣyasi
verses.essence: With mind fixed on me, you shall cross all difficulties by my grace. But if from egoism you will not listen, you shall perish.
verses.translation: Fixing thy mind on Me, thou shalt by My grace overcome all obstacles; but if from egoism thou wilt not hear Me, thou shalt perish.

anvaya.meaning: with mind on me, all hard passes
by my grace you shall cross
will not hear — you shall perish

verses.explanation: Promise and warning in perfect symmetry: mac-cittaḥ... tariṣyasi — mind in him, every durga, every fortress-like strait, crossed on grace; ahaṅkārāt... vinaṅkṣyasi — ego at the helm, ruin. The blunt second clause is love refusing to flatter: the stakes on this battlefield, and in every life, are real. The next verses show exactly *how* the ego's refusal would play out.
verses.connection: The fork stated plainly — and 18.59–60 walks down the refusal-branch to show where it leads.
verses.reflection: Stand at the fork consciously once today: mind-on-Him crosses everything; ego at the helm sinks.
verses.background: The teaching narrows to its personal close: doing all actions always, taking refuge in Me, by My grace one gains the eternal abode — so mentally resign all acts to Me and be ever-minded of Me: you shall cross every difficulty by My grace; but if from egoism you will not listen, you shall perish. Your own nature will compel what delusion now refuses; the Lord dwells in the heart of all beings, whirling them as if mounted on a machine. To Him surrender with your whole being. Thus the knowledge more secret than all secrets has been declared: reflect on it fully — then act as you choose.

verses.devanagari:
यदहंकारमाश्रित्य न योत्स्य इति मन्यसे ।
मिथ्यैष व्यवसायस्ते प्रकृतिस्त्वां नियोक्ष्यति॥
verses.transliteration:
yadahaṃkāramāśritya na yotsya iti manyase
mithyaiṣa vyavasāyaste prakṛtistvāṃ niyokṣyati
verses.essence: If, taking refuge in egoism, you think 'I will not fight' — vain is this resolve of yours: your own nature will compel you.
verses.translation: If, filled with egoism, thou thinkest: "I will not fight", vain is this, thy resolve; Nature will compel thee.

anvaya.meaning: 'I will not fight' — so you think
false is this resolve of yours

verses.explanation: The refusal-branch examined: na yotsye, 'I will not fight' — the very sentence of 2.9 — diagnosed as a decision made *by the ego about a nature it does not command*. Mithyā, void: a kshatriya's marrow, eighty years in the making, will not be vetoed by an afternoon's anguish. Prakṛtis tvāṁ niyokṣyati — what you are will overrule what you currently prefer.
verses.connection: Dismantles chapter 1's resolution with chapter 18's psychology: temperament outvotes mood.
verses.reflection: Catch one 'I will not' that your own nature is already overruling — and choose your relation to it.
verses.background: The teaching narrows to its personal close: doing all actions always, taking refuge in Me, by My grace one gains the eternal abode — so mentally resign all acts to Me and be ever-minded of Me: you shall cross every difficulty by My grace; but if from egoism you will not listen, you shall perish. Your own nature will compel what delusion now refuses; the Lord dwells in the heart of all beings, whirling them as if mounted on a machine. To Him surrender with your whole being. Thus the knowledge more secret than all secrets has been declared: reflect on it fully — then act as you choose.

verses.devanagari:
स्वभावजेन कौन्तेय निबद्धः स्वेन कर्मणा ।
कर्तुं नेच्छसि यन्मोहात्करिष्यस्यवशोपि तत्॥
verses.transliteration:
svabhāvajena kaunteya nibaddhaḥ svena karmaṇā
kartuṃ necchasi yanmohātkariṣyasyavaśopi tat
verses.essence: Bound by your own duty born of your nature, Kaunteya — what from delusion you wish not to do, that you shall do even against your will.
verses.translation: O Arjuna, bound by thy own Karma (action) born of thy own nature, that which from delusion thou wishest not to do, even that thou shalt do helplessly.

anvaya.meaning: by what is born of your own nature, Kaunteya
what, from delusion, you wish not to do
that you shall do, even helpless

verses.explanation: The argument completed without a single appeal to heaven: svabhāva-jena... nibaddhaḥ — bound by your own born nature, the war will pull you in avaśaḥ, will-or-no, once the first arrow flies past your brother. The only live choice is *how* you will be in it: dragged by compulsion, or upright in consecration. Freedom, the Gita has taught throughout, is not choosing your nature but choosing your relation to it.
verses.connection: Closes the compulsion-argument — and 18.61 reveals the deeper hand turning all natures.
verses.reflection: What you are will out — so consecrate it today rather than be dragged by it.
verses.background: The teaching narrows to its personal close: doing all actions always, taking refuge in Me, by My grace one gains the eternal abode — so mentally resign all acts to Me and be ever-minded of Me: you shall cross every difficulty by My grace; but if from egoism you will not listen, you shall perish. Your own nature will compel what delusion now refuses; the Lord dwells in the heart of all beings, whirling them as if mounted on a machine. To Him surrender with your whole being. Thus the knowledge more secret than all secrets has been declared: reflect on it fully — then act as you choose.

verses.devanagari:
ईश्वरः सर्वभूतानां हृद्देशेऽर्जुन तिष्ठति ।
भ्रामयन्सर्वभूतानि यन्त्रारूढानि मायया॥
verses.transliteration:
īśvaraḥ sarvabhūtānāṃ hṛddeśe'rjuna tiṣṭhati
bhrāmayansarvabhūtāni yantrārūḍhāni māyayā
verses.essence: The Lord dwells in the heart-region of all beings, Arjuna, whirling all beings by his maya, as if mounted on a machine.
verses.translation: The Lord dwells in the hearts of all beings, O Arjuna, causing all beings, by His illusive power, to revolve as if mounted on a machine.

anvaya.meaning: as if mounted on a machine, by maya

verses.explanation: The vista widens to its furthest edge: īśvaraḥ... hṛd-deśe tiṣṭhati — the Lord stationed in every heart, turning all beings yantrārūḍhāni, as figures mounted on a revolving engine, by his māyā. Nature itself, which 18.60 said compels you, is itself being turned. The image humbles utterly — and prepares the most tender pivot in the poem: if the Turner lives in your own heart, he can be *gone to*. Ishvarah sarva-bhutanam hrid-deshe 'rjuna tishthati — the Lord stands in the heart-region of all beings, turning them mounted as on a machine, by maya. The yantra image drew the tradition's longest meditations on freedom: Ramanuja resolves it — the Lord turns according to each soul's own karma and consent, the machinist honoring the rider's accumulated choices. The verse's geography is its gospel: the Turner is not above the machine but inside the rider — hence the very next word is 'go to Him.'
verses.connection: The final metaphysical frame: behind svabhava stands its Lord — making 18.62's surrender the obvious door.
verses.reflection: Remember mid-commotion: the Turner of all this machinery is seated in your own heart — visit Him there.
verses.background: The teaching narrows to its personal close: doing all actions always, taking refuge in Me, by My grace one gains the eternal abode — so mentally resign all acts to Me and be ever-minded of Me: you shall cross every difficulty by My grace; but if from egoism you will not listen, you shall perish. Your own nature will compel what delusion now refuses; the Lord dwells in the heart of all beings, whirling them as if mounted on a machine. To Him surrender with your whole being. Thus the knowledge more secret than all secrets has been declared: reflect on it fully — then act as you choose.

verses.devanagari:
तमेव शरणं गच्छ सर्वभावेन भारत ।
तत्प्रसादात्परां शान्तिं स्थानं प्राप्स्यसि शाश्वतम्॥
verses.transliteration:
tameva śaraṇaṃ gaccha sarvabhāvena bhārata
tatprasādātparāṃ śāntiṃ sthānaṃ prāpsyasi śāśvatam
verses.essence: To Him alone surrender with your whole being, Bharata. By His grace you shall attain supreme peace and the eternal abode.
verses.translation: Fly unto Him for refuge with all thy being, O Arjuna; by His grace thou shalt obtain supreme peace (and) the eternal abode.

anvaya.meaning: to Him alone go for refuge
with your whole being, O Bharata
by His grace, the supreme peace
and the eternal abode you shall attain

verses.explanation: The instruction the whole chapter has been narrowing toward: tam eva śaraṇaṁ gaccha — go to *Him* for refuge, sarva-bhāvena, with everything you are, holding nothing in reserve. Notice the third person: Krishna points away from himself to the Lord in the heart — the teacher's last humility before the final verses close the distance entirely. Grace answers wholeness: parā śānti, the peace this battlefield conversation began by seeking.
verses.connection: The penultimate instruction — whose first-person form, the Gita's last word, waits at 18.66.
verses.reflection: Surrender with the whole being once today — sarva-bhavena — nothing held back in reserve.
verses.background: The teaching narrows to its personal close: doing all actions always, taking refuge in Me, by My grace one gains the eternal abode — so mentally resign all acts to Me and be ever-minded of Me: you shall cross every difficulty by My grace; but if from egoism you will not listen, you shall perish. Your own nature will compel what delusion now refuses; the Lord dwells in the heart of all beings, whirling them as if mounted on a machine. To Him surrender with your whole being. Thus the knowledge more secret than all secrets has been declared: reflect on it fully — then act as you choose.

verses.devanagari:
इति ते ज्ञानमाख्यातं गुह्याद्गुह्यतरं मया ।
विमृश्यैतदशेषेण यथेच्छसि तथा कुरु॥
verses.transliteration:
iti te jñānamākhyātaṃ guhyādguhyataraṃ mayā
vimṛśyaitadaśeṣeṇa yathecchasi tathā kuru
verses.essence: Thus has this knowledge, more secret than all secrets, been declared to you by me. Reflect on it fully — then act as you choose.
verses.translation: Thus has wisdom, more secret than secrecy itself, been declared unto thee by Me; having reflected over it fully, then act as thou wishest.

anvaya.meaning: thus to you the knowledge has been declared
more secret than the secret, by me

verses.explanation: After seven hundred verses of the strongest counsel ever assembled, the teaching ends by handing back the wheel: vimṛśya etad aśeṣeṇa — think it through, *all* of it — and then four words that consecrate human freedom: yathecchasi tathā kuru, do as you choose. No scripture commands here; God himself declines to compel the heart he made free. The teacher's final gift is the dignity of the student's own decision. Iti te jnanam akhyatam — thus the knowledge has been declared, guhyad guhyataram, secret beyond secret — vimrishyaitad asheshena: reflect on it without remainder, then yathechchhasi tatha kuru — do as you choose. The commentaries stand bareheaded before this verse: revelation ends in liberty. The tradition reads vimrishya as the dignifying of reason within faith — the teaching asks to be thought through, not merely obeyed — and the granted freedom as the precondition of 18.73's freely chosen 'I will act.'
verses.connection: The release of the reins — the precondition for 18.73's freely-spoken 'I will act.'
verses.schools_note: Commentators across all schools pause in wonder here: revelation ends not in command but in liberty. The tradition reads it as the Gita's doctrine of grace perfected — love that will persuade everything and coerce nothing. The deliberation (vimrishya) is part of the instruction: faith is asked to pass through thought, not around it.
verses.reflection: Honor your own freedom as God honors it: deliberate fully today, then choose — yathecchasi tatha kuru.
verses.background: The teaching narrows to its personal close: doing all actions always, taking refuge in Me, by My grace one gains the eternal abode — so mentally resign all acts to Me and be ever-minded of Me: you shall cross every difficulty by My grace; but if from egoism you will not listen, you shall perish. Your own nature will compel what delusion now refuses; the Lord dwells in the heart of all beings, whirling them as if mounted on a machine. To Him surrender with your whole being. Thus the knowledge more secret than all secrets has been declared: reflect on it fully — then act as you choose.

verses.devanagari:
सर्वगुह्यतमं भूयः शृणु मे परमं वचः ।
इष्टोऽसि मे दृढमिति ततो वक्ष्यामि ते हितम्॥
verses.transliteration:
sarvaguhyatamaṃ bhūyaḥ śṛṇu me paramaṃ vacaḥ
iṣṭo'si me dṛḍhamiti tato vakṣyāmi te hitam
verses.essence: Hear again my supreme word, most secret of all. You are surely beloved of me — therefore I will speak for your good.
verses.translation: Hear thou again My supreme word, most secret of all; because thou art dearly beloved of Me, I will tell thee what is good.

anvaya.meaning: the most secret of all, again
you are firmly beloved of me — thus
therefore I will speak your good

verses.explanation: Having set Arjuna free, Krishna cannot quite stop — and the reason given is the whole theology of the Gita in one clause: iṣṭo 'si me dṛḍham, *you are firmly dear to me*. The final secret is not earned by qualification; it overflows from affection. What follows (18.65–66) is spoken not from the teacher's chair but from the friend's heart.
verses.connection: Love overriding the closure of 18.63 — the preface to the Gita's two culminating verses.
verses.reflection: Hear the reason behind every teaching you have received: you are firmly dear. Let that be the lens.
verses.background: What freedom released, love calls back: hear once more My supreme word, most secret of all — you are firmly beloved of Me, therefore I speak your good. Fix your mind on Me, be devoted, sacrifice, bow — you shall come to Me; truly I promise, you are dear to Me. Then the carama-shloka, the Gita's last teaching-word: abandoning all dharmas, take refuge in Me alone; I will release you from all sins — do not grieve.

verses.devanagari:
मन्मना भव मद्भक्तो मद्याजी मां नमस्कुरु ।
मामेवैष्यसि सत्यं ते प्रतिजाने प्रियोऽसि मे॥
verses.transliteration:
manmanā bhava madbhakto madyājī māṃ namaskuru
māmevaiṣyasi satyaṃ te pratijāne priyo'si me
verses.essence: Fix your mind on me, be devoted to me, sacrifice to me, bow to me — and you shall come to me alone. Truly I promise you: you are dear to me.
verses.translation: Fix thy mind on Me, by devoted to Me, sacrifice to Me, bow down to Me. Thou shalt come even to Me; truly do I promise unto thee, (for) thou art dear to Me.

anvaya.meaning: sacrificing to me — bow to me
to me alone you shall come — truly, to you
I promise: you are dear to me

verses.explanation: The fourfold heart-practice of 9.34 returns verbatim — mind, love, worship, reverence, all aimed at one address — but now sealed with what no earlier chapter carried: satyaṁ te pratijāne, *I promise you, truly*. The Absolute giving its word, and giving the reason: priyo 'si me. The whole apparatus of yoga compressed into a relationship between two who hold each other dear. Man-mana bhava mad-bhakto mad-yaji mam namaskuru — the four-fold heart-practice of 9.34, repeated here verbatim as the Gita nears its last word, now sealed with an oath: satyam te pratijane — truly I promise you — priyo 'si me, you are dear to Me. The commentaries note the unprecedented notarization: the Absolute giving its word, and giving the reason as affection. Madhusudana calls this the Gita's whole soteriology in one line: four movements of love, one promise, one motive.
verses.connection: 9.34 repeated and notarized — the promise that 18.66 will raise to its absolute form.
verses.schools_note: The verbatim repetition of 9.34 at the Gita's close is itself the commentary: of seven hundred verses, this fourfold practice is the one the text says twice. Madhusudana reads the added oath — satyam te pratijane — as the Lord placing His own truthfulness as collateral; the devotional schools recite the verse daily on exactly that security. Repetition plus promise equals, for the tradition, the Gita underlining its own essence.
verses.reflection: Do the four before sleep — mind on Him, love to Him, an offering, a bow — and rest on a promise.
verses.background: What freedom released, love calls back: hear once more My supreme word, most secret of all — you are firmly beloved of Me, therefore I speak your good. Fix your mind on Me, be devoted, sacrifice, bow — you shall come to Me; truly I promise, you are dear to Me. Then the carama-shloka, the Gita's last teaching-word: abandoning all dharmas, take refuge in Me alone; I will release you from all sins — do not grieve.

verses.devanagari:
सर्वधर्मान्परित्यज्य मामेकं शरणं व्रज ।
अहं त्वा सर्वपापेभ्यो मोक्षयिष्यामि मा शुचः॥
verses.transliteration:
sarvadharmānparityajya māmekaṃ śaraṇaṃ vraja
ahaṃ tvāṃ sarvapāpebhyo mokṣyayiṣyāmi mā śucaḥ
verses.essence: Abandoning all dharmas, take refuge in me alone. I will release you from all sins — do not grieve.
verses.translation: Abandoning all duties, take refuge in Me alone: I will liberate thee from all sins; grieve not.

anvaya.meaning: to me alone come for refuge

verses.explanation: The carama-śloka, the 'final verse' — the summit the tradition says the whole Gita climbs toward. Sarva-dharmān parityajya: release even the dharmas, the sacred scaffolding itself — not into lawlessness but into the One the laws were always pointing at. Then the only unconditional sentence in scripture: ahaṁ tvā sarva-pāpebhyo mokṣayiṣyāmi — *I* will free you, from *all* of it. And the words that answer the very first teaching: mā śucaḥ, grieve no more — the grief of chapter 1, finally and forever addressed. Sarva-dharman parityajya — the charama-shloka, the 'final verse' the Shri Vaishnava tradition holds so sacred it is transmitted at initiation with covered lips: abandon all dharmas, come to Me alone for refuge; I will release you from all sins — ma shuchah, do not grieve. Ramanuja's school reads it as the supreme means itself, prapatti, the Lord assuming the whole burden; Shankara as the renunciation of action in the knowledge of the actionless Self; the Gaudiyas as love's final call past every scaffold. The last two words answer the book's first wound — the grief of chapter one, dismissed forever by its Lord.
verses.connection: The Gita's last teaching-word: every path released into pure refuge — the answer to 1.47's weeping archer.
verses.schools_note: The most commented verse in the Gita. For Ramanuja's school it is the supreme means itself — prapatti, total surrender, with the Lord assuming all burden (some hold it so sacred it is whispered only at initiation). Shankara reads 'all dharmas' as the renunciation of all action in the knowledge of the actionless Self. Gaudiya teachers hear love's final call: beyond duty stands the Beloved. All agree: nothing further remains to be said.
verses.reflection: When every framework fails today, use the final verse as it was given: one refuge, all burdens lifted, grieve not.
verses.background: What freedom released, love calls back: hear once more My supreme word, most secret of all — you are firmly beloved of Me, therefore I speak your good. Fix your mind on Me, be devoted, sacrifice, bow — you shall come to Me; truly I promise, you are dear to Me. Then the carama-shloka, the Gita's last teaching-word: abandoning all dharmas, take refuge in Me alone; I will release you from all sins — do not grieve.

verses.devanagari:
इदं ते नातपस्काय नाभक्ताय कदाचन ।
न चाशुश्रूषवे वाच्यं न च मां योऽभ्यसूयति॥
verses.transliteration:
idaṃ te nātapaskāya nābhaktāya kadācana
na cāśuśrūṣave vācyaṃ na ca māṃ yo'bhyasūyati
verses.essence: This is never to be spoken by you to one without austerity, nor to one without devotion, nor to one unwilling to hear, nor to one who speaks ill of me.
verses.translation: This is never to be spoken by thee to one who is devoid of austerities or devotion, nor to one who does not render service or who does not desire to listen, nor to one who cavils at Me.

anvaya.meaning: this, by you, not to one without tapas
nor to be spoken to one not wishing to listen
nor to one who carps at me

verses.explanation: After the widest door, a guardian's word: the teaching is not to be pressed on the unready — the undisciplined, the loveless, the unwilling ear, the sneerer. Not elitism but ecology: seed scattered on scorn grows resentment, not freedom. The deepest things keep their own counsel until asked for; reverence is the soil this scripture germinates in.
verses.connection: The transmission-ethic: 18.66's open secret protected from contempt, kept for the thirsty.
verses.reflection: Guard the deepest things today — share them where there is thirst, not where there is sport.
verses.background: The transmission ethic is given: this is not to be spoken to one without austerity or devotion, to the unwilling ear, or the scoffer — but whoever declares this supreme secret among My devotees, showing highest love to Me, shall come to Me without doubt; none does Me dearer service, none on earth shall be dearer. Who studies this dialogue worships Me by the sacrifice of knowledge; even the faithful hearer, free of scoffing, is liberated to the bright worlds.

verses.devanagari:
य इदं परमं गुह्यं मद्भक्तेष्वभिधास्यति ।
भक्तिं मयि परां कृत्वा मामेवैष्यत्यसंशयः॥
verses.transliteration:
ya idaṃ paramaṃ guhyaṃ madbhakteṣvabhidhāsyati
bhaktiṃ mayi parāṃ kṛtvā māmevaiṣyatyasaṃśayaḥ
verses.essence: One who declares this supreme secret among my devotees, having shown highest devotion to me, shall come to me alone — there is no doubt.
verses.translation: He who with supreme devotion to Me will teach this supreme secret to My devotees, shall doubtlessly come to Me.

anvaya.meaning: having made the highest devotion to me
to me alone shall come — without doubt

verses.explanation: The flip side of the guard: among the thirsty, *pour*. To unfold this conversation for devoted hearts is itself ranked as parā bhakti, the highest act of love toward him — the teacher of the Gita performs devotion in the teaching. And the wage is the destination itself: mām evaiṣyati, with the chapter's recurring seal, asaṁśayaḥ — no doubt.
verses.connection: Sharing the teaching as worship — the verse behind every Gita discourse given since.
verses.reflection: Pass one verse of this book to a heart that needs it — the sharing is itself ranked as supreme love.
verses.background: The transmission ethic is given: this is not to be spoken to one without austerity or devotion, to the unwilling ear, or the scoffer — but whoever declares this supreme secret among My devotees, showing highest love to Me, shall come to Me without doubt; none does Me dearer service, none on earth shall be dearer. Who studies this dialogue worships Me by the sacrifice of knowledge; even the faithful hearer, free of scoffing, is liberated to the bright worlds.

verses.devanagari:
न च तस्मान्मनुष्येषु कश्चिन्मे प्रियकृत्तमः ।
भविता न च मे तस्मादन्यः प्रियतरो भुवि॥
verses.transliteration:
na ca tasmānmanuṣyeṣu kaścinme priyakṛttamaḥ
bhavitā na ca me tasmādanyaḥ priyataro bhuvi
verses.essence: Nor is there among humans any who does what is more dear to me than such a one; nor shall there be another on earth dearer to me.
verses.translation: Nor is there any among men who does dearer service to Me, nor shall there be another on earth dearer to Me than he.

anvaya.meaning: nor than that one, among humans
is any a doer of what is more dear to me
nor shall there be, than that one
another dearer to me on earth

verses.explanation: A double superlative the Lord gives no one else in the whole poem: priya-kṛt-tamaḥ — of all human beings, none does him a *dearer service* than the one who carries this dialogue to hearts that need it; and none, past or future, will be dearer. The Gita ranks its own transmission as the supreme act of love — because what is handed over is the way home itself.
verses.connection: The strongest commendation in the text — reserved for the sharer of 18.68.
verses.reflection: Know the value of the carrier: no dearer service exists than handing someone the way home.
verses.background: The transmission ethic is given: this is not to be spoken to one without austerity or devotion, to the unwilling ear, or the scoffer — but whoever declares this supreme secret among My devotees, showing highest love to Me, shall come to Me without doubt; none does Me dearer service, none on earth shall be dearer. Who studies this dialogue worships Me by the sacrifice of knowledge; even the faithful hearer, free of scoffing, is liberated to the bright worlds.

verses.devanagari:
अध्येष्यते च य इमं धर्म्यं संवादमावयोः ।
ज्ञानयज्ञेन तेनाहमिष्टः स्यामिति मे मतिः॥
verses.transliteration:
adhyeṣyate ca ya imaṃ dharmyaṃ saṃvādamāvayoḥ
jñānayajñena tenāhamiṣṭaḥ syāmiti me matiḥ
verses.essence: And one who studies this dharma-filled dialogue of ours — by that one I shall be worshiped through the sacrifice of knowledge. So I hold.
verses.translation: And he who will study this sacred dialogue of ours, by him I shall have been worshipped by the sacrifice of wisdom, such is My conviction.

anvaya.meaning: by the knowledge-sacrifice, by that one, I
shall be worshiped — such is my thought

verses.explanation: The promise widens to every future reader: saṁvādam āvayoḥ — *our* dialogue, the conversation itself sanctified as text — and its study declared a jñāna-yajña, the sacrifice of knowledge that 4.33 ranked above all offerings of substance. Whoever opens these chapters, in any century, in any tongue, is performing worship; the reading you are doing now is, by this verse, a rite.
verses.connection: Consecrates the act of reading the Gita itself — fulfilling 4.33's hierarchy of sacrifice.
verses.reflection: Read even one verse today as rite, not consumption — by this chapter, your reading is worship.
verses.background: The transmission ethic is given: this is not to be spoken to one without austerity or devotion, to the unwilling ear, or the scoffer — but whoever declares this supreme secret among My devotees, showing highest love to Me, shall come to Me without doubt; none does Me dearer service, none on earth shall be dearer. Who studies this dialogue worships Me by the sacrifice of knowledge; even the faithful hearer, free of scoffing, is liberated to the bright worlds.

verses.devanagari:
श्रद्धावाननसूयश्च शृणुयादपि यो नरः ।
सोऽपि मुक्तः शुभाँल्लोकान्प्राप्नुयात्पुण्यकर्मणाम्॥
verses.transliteration:
śraddhāvānanasūyaśca śṛṇuyādapi yo naraḥ
so'pi muktaḥ śubhā.Nllokānprāpnuyātpuṇyakarmaṇām
verses.essence: And even the one who, full of faith and without scoffing, simply hears it — that one too, liberated, shall attain the radiant worlds of the righteous.
verses.translation: Also the man who hears this, full of faith and free from malice, he, too, liberated, shall attain to the happy worlds of those of righteous deeds.

anvaya.meaning: who would even (only) hear — that person
that one too, freed, the auspicious worlds
would attain — of those of holy deeds

verses.explanation: The door opened to its widest: śṛṇuyād api — even one who only *hears*, asking nothing but faith and the absence of sneering. No Sanskrit, no study, no qualification: the grandmother at the recitation, the child half-listening at the lamp. So 'pi muktaḥ — that one *too* is carried. The scripture's last gradation of access dissolves every gradation: the lowest rung touches the same sky.
verses.connection: Completes the circle of 13.26: the path of the trusting ear, honored to the very end.
verses.reflection: If all you did today was listen without scoffing — that too is counted; the lowest rung touches the sky.
verses.background: The transmission ethic is given: this is not to be spoken to one without austerity or devotion, to the unwilling ear, or the scoffer — but whoever declares this supreme secret among My devotees, showing highest love to Me, shall come to Me without doubt; none does Me dearer service, none on earth shall be dearer. Who studies this dialogue worships Me by the sacrifice of knowledge; even the faithful hearer, free of scoffing, is liberated to the bright worlds.

verses.devanagari:
कच्चिदेतच्छ्रुतं पार्थ त्वयैकाग्रेण चेतसा ।
कच्चिदज्ञानसम्मोहः प्रनष्टस्ते धनञ्जय॥
verses.transliteration:
kaccidetacchrutaṃ pārtha tvayaikāgreṇa cetasā
kaccidajñānasammohaḥ pranaṣṭaste dhanañjaya
verses.essence: Has this been heard by you, Partha, with one-pointed mind? Has the delusion of your ignorance been destroyed, Dhananjaya?
verses.translation: Has this been heard, O Arjuna, with one-pointed mind? Has the delusion of thy ignorance been destroyed, O Dhananjaya?

anvaya.meaning: has this been heard, O Partha
been destroyed, of yours, O Dhananjaya

verses.explanation: The Teacher turns and asks — kaccit, the gentle interrogative of one who will not presume: did it reach you? whole? The question honors everything 18.63 established: the teaching cannot complete itself; only the hearer's own settled understanding can close the circuit. All of scripture waits, in the end, on one person's honest answer.
verses.connection: The exam of a single question — answered, after eighteen chapters, in 18.73.
verses.reflection: Answer the Teacher's question honestly tonight: did it reach me? Is anything of the fog gone?
verses.background: The Teacher turns to His student with the gentlest of examinations: has this been heard with one-pointed mind, O Partha — has the delusion of your ignorance been destroyed? And Arjuna gives the answer the whole poem exists to reach: destroyed is my delusion; memory is regained, by Your grace, O Achyuta. I stand firm, my doubts are gone — I will act according to Your word.

verses.speaker: अर्जुन उवाच
verses.devanagari:
नष्टो मोहः स्मृतिर्लब्धा त्वत्प्रसादान्मयाच्युत ।
स्थितोऽस्मि गतसन्देहः करिष्ये वचनं तव॥
verses.transliteration:
naṣṭo mohaḥ smṛtirlabdhā tvatprasādānmayācyuta
sthito'smi gatasandehaḥ kariṣye vacanaṃ tava
verses.essence: Arjuna says: my delusion is destroyed; memory is regained, Achyuta, by your grace. I stand firm, my doubts dispelled. I will act by your word.
verses.translation: Destroyed is my delusion as I have gained my knowledge (memory) through Thy grace, O Krishna. I remain freed from doubts. I will act according to Thy word.

anvaya.meaning: by your grace, by me, O Achyuta

verses.explanation: The destination of the entire poem, in four clauses: naṣṭo mohaḥ — the delusion that dropped the bow is gone; smṛtir labdhā — *memory regained*, not new information but the self remembered (the exact reversal of 2.63's chain, where memory's loss was ruin's last step); tvat-prasādāt — and he names the real agent: grace. Then the man who sat down stands: sthito 'smi. Kariṣye vacanaṁ tava — freely, as 18.63 allowed, he chooses the word he was never forced to obey. Nashto mohah smritir labdha — delusion destroyed, memory regained: the tradition reads labdha, regained, as the Gita's entire theory of liberation in one participle — nothing new was acquired; something native was remembered. Tvat-prasadat: and the agent named is grace. Sthito 'smi gata-sandehah, karishye vachanam tava — the commentaries note every clause answers chapter one point for point: the seated stands, the doubter is resolved, the deserter re-enlists — freely, under 18.63's granted liberty. This is what a completed teaching sounds like from the student's side.
verses.connection: Chapter 1's collapse answered point for point: the seated weeper stands, the cast-down bow is taken up — by choice.
verses.schools_note: Tradition reads 'smritir labdha' with great weight: liberation as remembering, not acquiring — the soul recalling its own nature, completing the arc begun at 2.63 where 'smriti-bhramshad buddhi-nasho' mapped the fall. Arjuna's 'karishye vachanam tava' is held up as the model response to revelation: surrender expressed as free, resolved action.
verses.reflection: Say the four when you are ready — delusion gone, memory regained, standing firm, I will act — and stand.
verses.background: The Teacher turns to His student with the gentlest of examinations: has this been heard with one-pointed mind, O Partha — has the delusion of your ignorance been destroyed? And Arjuna gives the answer the whole poem exists to reach: destroyed is my delusion; memory is regained, by Your grace, O Achyuta. I stand firm, my doubts are gone — I will act according to Your word.

verses.speaker: सञ्जय उवाच
verses.devanagari:
इत्यहं वासुदेवस्य पार्थस्य च महात्मनः ।
संवादमिममश्रौषमद्भुतं रोमहर्षणम्॥
verses.transliteration:
ityahaṃ vāsudevasya pārthasya ca mahātmanaḥ
saṃvādamimamaśrauṣamadbhutaṃ romaharṣaṇam
verses.essence: Sanjaya says: thus did I hear this wondrous dialogue of Vasudeva and the great-souled Partha — causing my hair to stand on end.
verses.translation: Thus I have heard this wonderful dialogue between Krishna and the high-souled Arjuna, which causes the hair to stand on end.
verses.explanation: The frame returns for the last time: Sanjaya, the seer-narrator, stepping forward to close what he opened at 1.1. His word for the whole: adbhutam roma-harṣaṇam — a wonder that raises the hair. We are reminded that everything since 'dharma-kṣetre' has passed through this one listening witness to a blind king — and now through him to us.
verses.connection: The outer frame closes: the epilogue of the witness (18.74–78) begins.
verses.reflection: Be a faithful Sanjaya today: carry one true conversation to someone who could not be in the room.
verses.background: The outer frame closes: Sanjaya, hair thrilling, tells the blind king what he has heard — this wondrous dialogue of Vasudeva and the great-souled Partha, received by Vyasa's grace from the Lord of yoga Himself — and confesses that remembering it again and again, and that most wondrous form, he rejoices again and again. His settled conviction ends the Gita: where Krishna the Lord of yoga is, and Partha the bowman, there are fortune, victory, prosperity, and unshakable right.

verses.devanagari:
व्यासप्रसादाच्छ्रुतवानेतद्गुह्यमहं परम् ।
योगं योगेश्वरात्कृष्णात्साक्षात्कथयतः स्वयम्॥
verses.transliteration:
vyāsaprasādācchrutavānetadguhyamahaṃ param
yogaṃ yogeśvarātkṛṣṇātsākṣātkathayataḥ svayam
verses.essence: By Vyasa's grace I heard this supreme secret, this yoga, directly from Krishna, the Lord of yoga himself, declaring it in person.
verses.translation: Through the grace of Vyasa I have heard this supreme and most secret Yoga direct from Krishna, the Lord of Yoga, Himself declaring it.

anvaya.meaning: this yoga, from Krishna the Lord of yoga
directly, as he himself declared it

verses.explanation: Sanjaya names his own grace-chain: vyāsa-prasādāt — the divine sight was Vyasa's gift — and through it, sākṣāt, *directly*, he heard the Lord of yoga speak. The verse quietly certifies the transmission every reader depends on: this text claims an unbroken line from the speaking mouth to the hearing ear to the written word to you.
verses.connection: The credential of the witness — grace upon grace carrying the dialogue out of the battlefield.
verses.reflection: Thank your own grace-chain tonight — every teacher and accident that carried this voice to your ear.
verses.background: The outer frame closes: Sanjaya, hair thrilling, tells the blind king what he has heard — this wondrous dialogue of Vasudeva and the great-souled Partha, received by Vyasa's grace from the Lord of yoga Himself — and confesses that remembering it again and again, and that most wondrous form, he rejoices again and again. His settled conviction ends the Gita: where Krishna the Lord of yoga is, and Partha the bowman, there are fortune, victory, prosperity, and unshakable right.

verses.devanagari:
राजन्संस्मृत्य संस्मृत्य संवादमिममद्भुतम् ।
केशवार्जुनयोः पुण्यं हृष्यामि च मुहुर्मुहुः॥
verses.transliteration:
rājansaṃsmṛtya saṃsmṛtya saṃvādamimamadbhutam
keśavārjunayoḥ puṇyaṃ hṛṣyāmi ca muhurmuhuḥ
verses.essence: O King, remembering again and again this wondrous and holy dialogue of Keshava and Arjuna, I rejoice again and again.
verses.translation: O King, remembering this wonderful and holy dialogue between Krishna and Arjuna, I rejoice again and again.
verses.explanation: The first testimony of the first reader: saṁsmṛtya saṁsmṛtya... muhur muhuḥ — the doubled words enacting what they describe, memory returning to the dialogue like a tongue to sweetness, joy renewing at each pass. Sanjaya models the relationship every generation since has had with this text: not read once, but remembered and remembered.
verses.connection: The witness's joy — the prototype of all Gita-remembrance, fulfilling 18.70's promise.
verses.reflection: Return to one passage that moved you and let it move you again — joy renews at each remembering.
verses.background: The outer frame closes: Sanjaya, hair thrilling, tells the blind king what he has heard — this wondrous dialogue of Vasudeva and the great-souled Partha, received by Vyasa's grace from the Lord of yoga Himself — and confesses that remembering it again and again, and that most wondrous form, he rejoices again and again. His settled conviction ends the Gita: where Krishna the Lord of yoga is, and Partha the bowman, there are fortune, victory, prosperity, and unshakable right.

verses.devanagari:
तच्च संस्मृत्य संस्मृत्य रूपमत्यद्भुतं हरेः ।
विस्मयो मे महान् राजन्हृष्यामि च पुनः पुनः॥
verses.transliteration:
tacca saṃsmṛtya saṃsmṛtya rūpamatyadbhutaṃ hareḥ
vismayo me mahān rājanhṛṣyāmi ca punaḥ punaḥ
verses.essence: And remembering again and again that most wondrous form of Hari, great is my astonishment, O King — and I rejoice again and again.
verses.translation: And, remembering again and again, also that most wonderful form of Hari, great is my wonder, O King; and I rejoice again and again.

anvaya.meaning: the form most wondrous of Hari
great is my amazement, O King
and I rejoice, again and again

verses.explanation: Of all that was spoken, what returns unbidden to Sanjaya is what was *shown*: the viśvarūpa of chapter 11, atyadbhutam, exceeding wonder — for he too saw it, by his borrowed sight. Vismayo me mahān: amazement that has not faded a single degree by the telling's end. Some things, once seen, see you ever after.
verses.connection: The vision of ch11 echoing in the closing frame — the sight that outlasts all the words.
verses.reflection: Keep one vision that once stunned you unfaded — revisit it until the amazement reports back.
verses.background: The outer frame closes: Sanjaya, hair thrilling, tells the blind king what he has heard — this wondrous dialogue of Vasudeva and the great-souled Partha, received by Vyasa's grace from the Lord of yoga Himself — and confesses that remembering it again and again, and that most wondrous form, he rejoices again and again. His settled conviction ends the Gita: where Krishna the Lord of yoga is, and Partha the bowman, there are fortune, victory, prosperity, and unshakable right.

verses.devanagari:
यत्र योगेश्वरः कृष्णो यत्र पार्थो धनुर्धरः ।
तत्र श्रीर्विजयो भूतिर्ध्रुवा नीतिर्मतिर्मम॥
verses.transliteration:
yatra yogeśvaraḥ kṛṣṇo yatra pārtho dhanurdharaḥ
tatra śrīrvijayo bhūtirdhruvā nītirmatirmama
verses.essence: Where there is Krishna, the Lord of yoga, and where there is Partha, the bearer of the bow — there abide fortune, victory, prosperity and unshakable justice. So I hold.
verses.translation: Wherever is Krishna, the Lord of Yoga; wherever is Arjuna, the wielder of the bow; there are prosperity, victory, happiness and firm policy; such is my conviction.

anvaya.meaning: where the Lord of yoga, Krishna
and steadfast right — such is my conviction

verses.explanation: The Gita's last verse, and the tradition's benediction ever since: not a doctrine but a *conjunction* — yatra... yatra... tatra: where the Lord of yoga and the bow-bearing human stand together, there fortune, victory, abundance and unswerving dharma are certain. Grace alone does not fight; effort alone does not see; joined, they are invincible. The blind king asked at 1.1 what happened on the field of dharma. After seven hundred verses, Sanjaya answers with where to place one's hope: wherever those two ride in one chariot — which may be any heart, on any morning, that takes up its bow beside its God. Yatra yogeshvarah krishno yatra partho dhanur-dharah — where the Lord of yoga and the bow-bearing human stand together: there fortune, victory, prosperity, unshakable right. The tradition reads the final verse as the Gita's siddhanta delivered as benediction: neither grace without effort nor effort without grace, but the two in one chariot. Commentators note it answers 1.1 — the blind king asked what happened on the field; the seer answers with where to look forever after: dhruva nitir matir mama, this is my settled conviction.
verses.connection: The closing benediction: divine guidance and human resolve in one chariot — the whole Gita compressed into its final image, answering the question of 1.1.
verses.schools_note: The benediction's two names are read as the Gita's final equation: Yogeshvara (grace, the Lord of all means) and Dhanurdhara (effort, the human with bow in hand) — Madhusudana calls their conjunction the siddhanta itself: neither divine will without human readiness nor valor without the divine charioteer. Vaishnava liturgy closes recitations with this verse for that reason: it is the whole teaching folded into a blessing.
verses.reflection: Close with the benediction's geometry: put the divine and your own bow in one chariot today — and trust the outcome.
verses.background: The outer frame closes: Sanjaya, hair thrilling, tells the blind king what he has heard — this wondrous dialogue of Vasudeva and the great-souled Partha, received by Vyasa's grace from the Lord of yoga Himself — and confesses that remembering it again and again, and that most wondrous form, he rejoices again and again. His settled conviction ends the Gita: where Krishna the Lord of yoga is, and Partha the bowman, there are fortune, victory, prosperity, and unshakable right.
